On  the  Management  of  the  Teeth. 

The  principal  requisite  for  the  .preserv«tio)»  of  Ae  teeth 
18,  never  to  retire  tp  .  rest  without  having '  cleaned  them* 
Thus,  the  vicious  matter  of  food  collected  during  the  day, 
cannot  corrupt  the  teeth  in  the  night  The  toothache  now  so 
common,  is  indeed  frequently  owing  to  a  hoUow  state  of  the 
teeth ;  but  still  oftener  it  originates  in  a  want  of  cleanliness* 
The  cleaning  of  the  teeth,  however,  requires  precaution. 
What  is  called  tartar  of  ^  the  teeth  is  of  a  corrosive  nature,  and 
should  be  removed  with  the  greatest  care.  The  manner  in 
which  itinerant  dentists  usually  treat  the  teeth,  as  well  as  theiv 
powders,  tinctures,  and  other  dentrifices,  however  highly  puQed 
off  and  strongly  reconunended,  are  obviously  pernicious. 

If  there  be  too  much  tartar,  so  that  it  adheres  like  a  cement 
between  two  teeth,  it  being  iocautioualy  removed  will  deprive 
the  teeth  of  the  tartarous  cohesion,  and  consequently  of  theii; 
support;  and  from  the  constant  contact  of* the  tongue,  lips^ 
and  food,  they  will  be  shaken  and  loosened.  The  same  will 
l^pen,  should  the  tartar  be  allowed  to  eat  away  the  ^m 
from  the  root  of  the  tooth.  If  in  this  case  the  fonndatian  of 
the  tooth  be  injured,  such  a  tooth  will  necessarily  be  rendere4 
loose :  the  gums  will  be  able  no  loqger  to  keep  a  tooth,  which 
is  deprived  of  its  intermediate  cement. 

The  tartar  therefore  must  not  be  broken,  all  at  once^  with 
iron  or  glass  instruxqents;  but  may  be  gradually  scraped  away 
with  a  blunt  or  broad  cut  quill,  or  some  similar  substance  fnH« 
which  the  enamel  of  the  teeth  will  suffer  no  ixgury.  Most 
kind  of  dissolved  drops,  especially  those  sold  aa  specifics  for 
whitening  the  tejetb,  are  made  up  of  vitriolic  acid^  diluted  with 
some  distilled  waters.  They  are  of  no  iiervioe :  on  the  contrary, 
they  remove  the  enamel  with  the  tartar,  and  thus  ^oU  the 
teeth  for  ever.  The  common  tooth-brushes  are  liable  to  the 
same  objection. 

To  .prevent  the  tartar  from  settling  on  the  teeth,  they  ought 
to  be  kept  clean,  by  washing  them  every  morning  and  evening. 
An  equally  safe,  and  as  effectual  an  expedient,  for  removing 
the  tartar  is,  to  cover  th^  teeth  with  a  fine  powder  of  gmn 
tragacanth,  or  with  soft  wax,  and  thi}S  to  extract  the  tartar  at 
once,  tggjsther  with  this  covering. 


M8  DOMBBTIC  COOKBRT. 

In  scorbutic  affections  of  the  teeth  and  gums,  a  vegetaUe 
diet  consisting  chiefly  of  ripe  fruit  and  mucilaginous  vegeta- 
bles, will  be  fb^nd  the  best  correctives.  Beside  these,  a  fin« 
powder,  made  of  three  parts  of  double-refined  sugar,  and  one 
part  of  burnt  alum,  may  be  employed  with  advantage  for  the 
purpose  of  rubbing  them. — To  dissolve  and  wash  away  the 
superfluous,  sHmy,  and  unctuous' matters  which  produce  the 
tartar,  fresh  water  is  sufficient,  though  it  may  be  rendered  a 
little  more  acrid  by  the  admixture  of  a  small  quantity  of 
common  sah.— The  most  simple  dentifHoe  is  a  crust  of  bread 
hard  toasted,  and  reduced  to  a  fine  powder.  This  is  fuUy 
calculated  to  absorb  the  viscid,  oleagimms  particles,  and  to 
remove  the  stony  or  tartarous  matter.  The  bread,  however, 
should  not  be  roasted  too  black,  as  in  that  case  it  would  evolve 
an  acrid  alkaline  salt,  which  might  prove  hurtful.  A  still 
better  dentifrice  is  a  moderatdy  fine  powder  of  the  Peruvian 
bark,  particularly  of  the  genuine  red 'species,  which  strengthens 
the  gums,  without  inflaming  them. 

In  cleaning  the  teeth  we  ought  not  to  make  use  of  brushes 
or  spudges,  but  of  the  finger,  which  being  provided  with  the 
finest  papillary  vessels,  is  a  much  better  and  more  proper 
instrument,  and  precludes  the  necessity  of  resorting  to  artificial 
means.  Besicfos,  the  finger  has  the  advantage  of  being  soft, 
and  pliable,  and  of  feeling  any  immoderate  pressure  too  sensi- 
bly, to  permit  us  to  do  injury  to  the  teeth  or  gums :  hence  it 
is  an  ill  understood  delicacy  alone,  which  can  prevent  us  fi'om 
making  use  of  it,  instead  of  even  the  best  Parisian  tooth-brushes. 
For  cleaning  the  interstices  between  the  teedi,  we  should  not 
employ  pins  or  needles,  whether  made  of  gold,  silver,  or  steel; 
for  all  metsllic  substances  are  apt  to  canker  the  teeth.  If 
toothpicks  be  at  all  adviseable,  they  should  be  made  of  soft 
wood,  or  quills  cut  in  a  blunt  point.  To  answer  every  purpose 
of  toothpicks,  a  thick  and  soft  cotton  doth  should  be  used,  to 
rub  the  teeth  over  gently  after  every  meal:  but  if  people  have 
once  accustomed  themselves  to  regularly  picking  their  teeth, 
dien  indeed  the  cotton  firictiohs  may  perhaps  be  too  late. 

Lastly,  the  ckaning  and  brudiing  of  the  teeth,  however 
useful  and  necessary,  is  sufficient  to  prevent  the  settling  of 
the  tartar,  and  the  consequent  injury  to  the  teeth ;  for  the 
source  of  both  evils  does  not  exist  in  the  mouth,  but  really 


FAMILY  PHYSICIAN.  66^ 

proceeds  from  the  stomach,  and  a  corrupted  state  of  the  fluids. 
Hence  the  medical  treatment  of  the  teeth  requires  a  particular 
regimen  and  diet^  according  to  the  individual  case  of  every 
patient. 

On  the  Treatment  and  Preservation  of  the  Eyes^ 

There  is  no  part  of  the  body  that  is  more  essential  for 
constituting  personal  beauty,  or  that  cpntributeth  more  to  our 
comforts,  than  the  eye.  Hence  the  management  of  the  eyea 
deserves  the  care  and  attention  of  eyery  female  who  wishes  to 
preserve  her  beauty  and  usefulness. 

Those  who  are  natura^y  short-sigl^ted  are  entitled  to  expect 
an  improvement  of  vision  with  the  advancement  of  age;  for 
their  eyes  then  gradually  begin  to  lose  that  uncommon  round- 
ness which  produces  this  defect,  and  thus  to  arrive  at  a  greater 
enjoyment  of  the  view  of  the  beauties  of  nature.  Persons 
who  can  see  objects  distinctly  at  a  great  distance  only,  cannot, 
however,  be  considered  as  less  unfortunate ;  as  they  stand  iiv 
need  of  glasses,  merely  for  the  sake  of  better  distinguishing 
the  more  minute  objects. 

Short-sighted  people  ought  not  to  indulge  the  bad  habit  of 
using  one  eye  only :  and  they  should  endeavour  to  read  with 
the  book  held  at  some  distance  straight  before  them.  It  is  a 
CQn^latioo  in  many  diseases  of  the  eyes,  that  a  long-continued. 
"W^kpeids  is  seldom  the  fore-runner  of  tQtal  blindness.  This 
fa^l  .event  generally  happens  by  sudden  accidents,  and  is 
speedily  decide(l.— Adults  are  not  very  subject  to  external 
complaints  of  the  eye,  or  such  as  deprive  the  cornea  of  its, 
transparency.  Small  round  spots,  hovering  before  the  eyes 
like  strings  pf  hollow  little  globules  connected  with  one  another, 
are  defers  of  no  great  omsequence,  and  of  which  perhaps  no 
eye  is  completely  free. 

It  is  a -very  bad  pri^ctice  tP  ?ndei^vour  to  protect  weak  eyes- 
by  means  of  shades.  Darkness  or  sh^de  is  only  beneficial  tQ 
the  eyps,  when  they  are  unemployed,  when  the  obscurity  is 
natural,  consequently  ^very  where  ext^nd^d.  To  rest  a  little 
during  the  twilight  i)»  very  suitable  to  weak  eyes«  No  artificial 
darkness  during  the  d^^y  is  ever  so  ^niform,  but  that  the  eye 
mjust  ^xert  itself  at  one  time  more  than  at  another,  and  neces- 
sarily suffer  by  this  change.  Persons  with  weak  or  diseased 
21  4d 


eye*,  who  ipend  the  whole  dftf  in  an  apwtment  darkened  w'ltli 
green  cartaina,  much  iajnve  tbemselres  by  this  pemicioaB  prac- 
tice. It  is  &r  preferable  to  repair  to  dear  day-light  and  the 
freth  air,  and  to  direct  the  eye  to  a  dictant  proRpcct,  than  to 
confine  them  to  the  doie  aimosph»«  of  a  room,  and  to  the 
tight  of  near  objects. 

Lastly,  it  is  an  error,  that  weak  eyes,  when  employed  in 
minute  vision,  ought  to  have  a  faint  light :  by  this  practice 
they  are  certainly  still  niore  weakened.  Thus  green  spectacles 
are  very  hurtful  to  tome  eyes,  as  they  deprive  them  of  that 
light  which  t<  necetsary  to  distinct  vision. 

The  artificial  light  of  candles  and  lamps  is,  npon  the  whole, 
detrimental  to  weak  eyes.  The  study-lamps  with  large  roond 
Bcreens,  and  also  the  green  parchment  screen,  are  very  objec- 
tionable. The  best  and  most  proper  defence  of  weak  eyes 
by  candle-ligbt  is  a  flat  screen,  projecting  about  two  or  three 
inches  over  the  forehead  ;  or  even  a  round  bat,  with  a  brim  of 
a  proper  sice.  The  tandle  should  always  be  so  placed,  tbsC 
the  flame  be  neither  too  low,  nor  too  much  above  the  height  of 
the  eye.  But  gas-lights  are  superior  to  others,  as  thecolonrand 
ateadiness  of  the  light  are  soothing  to  the  eye. 

In  the  early  morning  we  should  not  too  much  exeit  the 
eyes  immediately  after  rising.  Hence  it  is  adviseable  to  re- 
move the  candle  to  some  distance  and  under  shade,  in  the  loty 
winter  mornings,  till  the  eye  be  gradually  accustomed  to  it. 
For  the  same  reaaoo,  the  window  shutters  ought  not  to  be 
suddenly  opened  in  very  clear  day-light.  This  imwMMliHtf 
change,  from  darkness  to  the  clearest  light,  occasions  s^tuible 
pain  even  to  the  strongest  eye. 

Every  exertion  of  the  eyes  is  most  hurtful  immediately  afW 
«  meal,  as  well  as  at  any  time  when  the  blood  is  in  great  agita- 
ticra. — In  the  dawn,  in  twilight,  and  in  moonshine,  we  ought 
not  toreador  write,  nor  to  direct  our  sight  attentiTel/ta  objects 
whether  near  or  distant. 

Those  who  bare  weak  eyes  should  carefully  avoid  stm^ 
fires  and  even  hot  rooms ;  for  heat  still  man  dries  the  eyes 
already  suffering  &am  want  of  moisture.  Indeed,  it  is  highly 
probable,  that  die  weakoesa  of  sight  and  eariy  Uindness,  so 
common  in  this  country,  are  in  a  great  measure  owiag  to  the 


FAMILY  PHYSICIAN.  671 

bad  custom  of  hastening  to  the  fireside,  whether  coming  from 
tile  cold  air,  or  from  the  dark  streets. 

If  it  becomes  necessary  to  let  the  ejes  rest,  we  should  bj 
no  means  press  the  eye-lids  too  closely  together,  which,  if  long 
continued,  is  very  hurtful.  As  a  protection  against  injury 
from  external  causes,  it  is  most  useful  to  wear  a  shade  at  such 
a  distance  as  may  allow  the  eye  free  motion,  and  not  keep  it  too 
warm.  The  green  veils  worn  by  ladies  are,  in  this  respect, 
well  calculated  to  prevent  the  dust  from  entering  the  eye,  as 
well  as  to  protect  it  against  the  cold  winds,  and  the  burning 
rays  of  the  sun. 

Of  all  the  remedies  for  preserving  weak  eyes,  (for  diseased 
eyes  require  professional  assistance)  bathing  them  in  pure  cold 
water  is  the  most  refreshing  and  strengthening.  But  this  ought 
not  to  be  done  above  three  or  four  times  a  day  ;  otherwise 
it  has  a  tendency  to  give  an  unnecessary  stimulus  to  the  eyes. 
Nor  should  it  be  done  immediately  after  rising  in  the  morning, 
but  only  when  the  moisture,  which  during  sleep  is  deposited 
even  in  the  soundest  eyes,  is  nearly  evaporated.  This  partial 
cold  bath  may  be  repeated  after  dinner  and  supper,  at  which 
times  the  eyes  stand  as  much  in  need  of  it  as  in  the  morning*^ 
Not  only  the  eyes,  but  also  the  brow,  the  region  behind  the 
ears,  sometimes  the  whole  head,  and  particularly  the  upper 
Up,  which  is  closely  connected  with  the  optic  nerves,  should 
be  bathed  or  washed  as  well  as  the  eyes.  In  the  morning, 
the  eye  ought  not  to  be  precipitately,  but  gradually  exposed 
to  the  water;  and  the  washing  should  be  expeditiously  per- 
formed, in  drying  or  wiping  the  eye,  we  should  proceed 
gently  and  carefully.  Immediately  after  washing,  we  should 
particularly  guard  against  any  rays  of  light,  as  well  as  every 
kind  of  exertion. 

A  large  piece  of  sponge,  which  contains  a  good  deal  of 
water,  so  that  it  may  not  too  soon  become  warm,  is  far  prefer- 
able in  these  partial  bathings,  to  the  warm  smooth  hand  or 
towel.  The  sponge  should  be  frequently  dipped  in  cold  water, 
and  occasionally  allowed  to  lie  for  a  few  moments  on  the  eye. 
with  the  head  bent  backwards,  while  the  eye  is  gently  moved 
and  a  little  opened  during  the  application  of  the  sponge. 

Smoking  tobacco,  and  taking  snuff,  are  injurious;  as  by 
practice  the  eye  is  too  mudi  stimulated.    The  state  of

the  weather  alio  greatly  inflaences  the  power  of  vision.  Per- 
sons troubled  with  weak  eyes  should  therefore  not  be  alarmed, 
ff  in  a  tempest  or  thunder-storm^  in  rainy,  ^^^£»7  weather,  their 
sight  be  less  acute.  Such  individuals  are  easily  affected  by 
standing  too  long  on  cold  or  damp  ground,  by  too  light  dress, 
and  particularly  by  too  thin  covering  of  the  legs  and  feet. 

Riding  on  horseback  is  beneficial  to  weak  eyes,  as  is  also 
walking  and  riding  in  carriages.  The  pirincipal  advantage  in 
all  these  exercises  is,  perhaps,  derived  from  employing  the  eye 
with  a  great  variety  of  objects,  none  of  which  occupies  our 
attention  too  long. 

Lastly,  persons  having  black  eye-lashes  generally  possess 
greater  powers  of  vision,  than  those  whose  eye-lashes  are  of  a 
light  colour  ;  because  the  former  affords  a  better  screen  for  the 
eye,  and  reflect  no  light  from  their  outside,  by  which  the  image 
on  the  retina  could  be  rendered  weaker  and  more  indistinct. 

As  our  instructions  are  directed  to  young  females  only, 
it  is  not  necessary  to  give  rules  respecting  the  choice  and  use 
of  eye-glasses.  These  ought  .^ways  to  be  used  with  caution, 
as  many  good  eyes  have  been  spoiled  by  the  use  of  improper 
<ones,  particularly  su(9li  as  have  strong  magnifying  powers. 

On  Patent  and  Quack  Medicines. 

Although  there  is  but  one  state  of  perfect  health,  yet  the 
deviations  from  it,  and  tlie  genera  and  species  of  diseases,  are 
almost  infinite.  It  will  hence,  without  difficulty,  be  understood, 
that  in  the  classes  of  medical  remedies  there  must  be  likewise 
a  great  variety,  and  even  some  of  them  of  opposite  tendencies. 
It  is  evident  from  these  premises,  that  an  universal  remedy,  or 
one  diat  possesses  healing  powers  for  the  cure  of  all  diseases, 
is  in  fact  a  nonentity,  the  existence  of  which  is  physically  im- 
possible ;  as  the  bare  idea  of  it  involves  a  direct  contradiction. 

Indeed,  the  belief  in  an  universal  remedy  appears  to  lose 
ground  every  day,'  even  among  the  vulgar,  and  has  been  long 
exploded  in  those  classes  of  society,  which  are  not  influenced 
by  prejudice,  and  not  tinctured  with  fanaticism.  It  is,  how- 
ever, sincerely  to  be  regretted,  that  we  are  still  inundated  with 
a  flood  of  advertisements  in  almost  every  paper ;  that  the  lower 
and  less  enlightened  classes  of  the  community  are  still  imposed 
upon  by  a  set  of  privileged  impostors,  who  frequently  puizle 


•r« 


FAMlLTf   PHYSICTAN.  6W 

the  intelligent  reader  to  decide^  whether  the  boldness  or  the 
industry  with  which  they  endeavour  to  establish  the  reputation 
of  their  respective  poisons^  be  the  most  prominent  featare  iik 
their  character. — It  was  justly  observed  by  the  sagacious  and 
'Comprehensive  Bacon,  '  that  a  reflecting  physician  is  not  direct- 
ed by  the  opinion  which  the  multitude  entertain  of  a  favourite 
remedy;  but  that  he  must  be  guided  by  a  sound  judgment^ 
and  consequently  he  is  led  to  make  very  important  distinc- 
tions between  those  things,  which  only  by  their  name  pass 
for  medical  remedies,  and  others  which  in  reality  possess  heal- 
ing powers.  ' ' ' 

This  quotation  indirectly  censures  the  conduct  of  certain 
medical  practitioners,  who  do  not  scruple  to  recommend  what 
are  vulgarly  called  patent   and    other  quack  medicines,    the 
composition  of  which  is  eareftdly  concealed  from  the  public. 
Having  acquired  their  Ill-merited  reputation  by  mere  chance, 
and  being  supported  by  the  most  refined  artifices  to  delude  the 
unwary,  we  are  unable  to  come  at  the  evidence  of  perhaps 
nine-tenths  of  those  who  have  experienced  their  fatal  effects, 
and  who  are  now  no  longer  in  a  situation  to  complain.     The 
transition  from  panaceas,  or  universal  remedies,  to  nostrums, 
or  specifics,  such,  for  instance,  as  are  pretended  to  cure  the 
same  disease  in  even/  patient,  is  easy  and  natural.      With  the 
latter  also,    impositions  of  a  dangerous  tendency  are  often 
practised.    It  will  probably  be  asked  here.  How  far  are  they 
practically  admissible,  and  in  what  cases  ate  they  wholly  un- 
availing ?     It  is  not  very  difficult  to  answer  this  question.     In 
those  diseases,  which  in  every  instance  depend  upon  the  same 
cause,  as  in  agues,  the  small  pox,  measles,  ^d  many  other 
contagions  distempers,  the  possibility  of  specifics,  in  a  limited 
sense,  may  be  rationally,   though  hypotheticatly,    admitted. 
But  in  other  maladies,  the  causes  of  which  depend  upon  a 
variety  of  concurrent  circumstances,  and  the  cure  of  which,  in 
different  individuals^  frequently  requires  very  opposite  reme- 
dies, as  in  the  diropsy,  the  various  species  of  colic,  the  almost 
infinite  variety  of  consumptions,  &c.  a  specific  remedy  is  an 
insolent  burlesque  upon  t!he  common  sense  of  mankind.   Those 
who  are  but  imperfectly  acquainted  with  the  various  causes 
from  which  the  same  disorder  originates  in  different  individuals, 
can  never  entertaiii  such  a  vulgar  an4  dangerous  notion.    They 


674  DOMESTIC  COOKBAY. 

will  easily  perceive,  how  mudi  depends  upon  asoeitaiiiing  witk 
precision  the  seat  and  cause  of  the  affection,  before  any  medi- 
cine can  be  prescribed  with  advantage  or  safety  ;'-even  life  and 
death,  says  a  medical  writer,  are  too  often  decided  by  the 
fi'st  steps  of  him,  who  offers  or  intrudes  his  advice  upon  a 
suffering  friend. 

The  following  instances  will  show  the  danger  attending  the 
precipitate  application  of  the  same  medicine  in  similar  disorders. 
— A  person  violently  troubled  with  the  colic  took  a  glass  of 
juniper  spirits,    commonly  called   Hollands,,  from  which   be 
received  almost  instantaneous  relief,  as  the  afi'ection  proceeded 
from  flatulency.    Another  person,  who  fooncf  Jiiniaelf  attacked 
with  similar  pains,  was  induced  by  the  example  of  his  friend 
to  try  the  same  expedient ;  he  took  it  without  hesitation,  and 
died  in  a  few  hours  after.     No  wonder  that  the  consequences 
here  were  fatal,  as  the  colic  in  the  latter  case  was  owing  .to  an 
inflammi^tion  in  the  intestines. — A  third  person  was  afflicted 
with  a  colic,  arising  from  poisonous  mushrooms  which  he  had 
inadvertently  swallowed ;  the  immediate  administration  of  an 
emetic,  and  after  it  some  diluted  vegetable  acid,  restored  him 
to  health.    A  fburth  person  had  an  attack  of  this  malady  from 
an  encysted  hernia,  or  an  inward  rupture.     The  emetic,  whidi 
relieved  the  former  patient,   necessarily  proved  &tal  to  the 
latter ;  for  it  burst  the  bag  of  enclosed  matter,  poured  the 
contents  within  the  cavities  of  the  abdomen,  and  thus  speedily 
terminated  his  existence. — Again,    another  had  by  mistake 
made  use  of  arsenic,  which  occasicmed  violent  pains,  not  unlike 
those  of  a  common  colic.    A  large  quantity  of  sweet  oil  takai 
internally  was  the  means  of  his  preservation;    whtfeas  the 
remedies  employed  in  the  other  cases  would  have  been  totally 
ineffectual.     Here  we  willingly  close  a  narrative,  the  recital 
of  which  cannot  but  excite  the  most  painful  sensatiois :   to 
lengthen  this  illustration  would  lead  us  too  far  beyond  our 
prescribed  limits;  since  cases  of  this  nature  happen  so  fre* 
quently,  that  it  would  be  easy  to  extend  the  account  of  them, 
by  a  long  catalogue  of  interesting  but  fktsX  accidents. 

What  is  more  natural  than  to  place  confidence  in  a  temeij, 
which  we  have  known  to  afford  relief  to  others  in  the  same  kind 
of  affection  >  The  patient  anxiously  inquires  after  a  peraao, 
who  has  been  afflicted  with  the  same  malady.     She  is  eager  to 


VAMIIiY  PBT8ICIAN.  575 

learn  the  remedy  that  has  been  used  with  saccefs.  Her  friend 
or  neighbour  imparts  to  her  the  wiahed-for  intelligence.  She 
is  determined  to  give  it  a  fair  trial,  and  takes  it  with  confidence. 
From  what  has  been  stated,  it  will  not  be  difficult  to  conceive, 
that  if  her  case  does  not  exactly  correspond  with  that  of  her 
friend,  any  chance  remedy  may  be  extremely  dangerous,  and 
even  fiital. 

One  of  the  most  unfortunate  circumstances  in  the  history  of 
such  medicines  is  the  insinuating  and  dangerous  method  by 
which  they  are  puffed  into  notice.  And  as  we  hear  little  of 
the  baneful  effects  which  they  daily  produce,  by  being  pro- 
miscuously applied,  people  attend  only  to  the  extraordinary 
mstances,  perhaps  not  one  in  fifty,  where  they  have  afforded  a 
temporary  or  apparent  relief.  It  is  well  known  that  the  more 
powerful  a  remedy  is,  the  more  permanent  and  dangerous  must 
be  its  effects  on  the  constitution ;  especially  if  it  be  introduced 
like  many  patent  medicines,  by  an  almost  indefinite  increase  of 
the  doses.— An  Italian  count,  uncommonly  fond  of  swallowing 
medicines,  found  at  length  that  he  could  take  no  more.  Pre- 
viously to  his  death,  he  ordered  the  following  inscription  to 
be  placed  on  his  tomb:  I  was  once  healthy ;  I  wished  to  be 
better  ; '  /  took  medicine,  and  died. 

A  popular  and  judicious  writer.  Dr.  Buchan,  makes  the 
following  curious  remark  on  the  subject  in  question:  'As 
matters  stand  at  present,*  says  he,  'it  is  easier  to  cheat  a  man 
out  of  his  life  than  of  a  shilling,  and  almost  impossible  either 
to  detect  or  punish  the  offender.  Notwithstanding  this,  people 
still  shut  their  eyes,  and  take  every  thing  upon  trust  that  is 
administered  by  any  pretender  to  medicine,  witho'Ut  daring  to 
ask  him  a  reason  for  any  part  of  his  conduct.  Implicit  fiuth, 
every  where  else  the  object  of  ridicule,  is  still  sacred  here.' 

Summary  of  the  preceding  Rukt. 

Having  warned  our  fair  readers  from  being  deluded  by  the 
impudent  and  ruinous  pretensions  of  quacks,  we  shall  now  give 
a  few  plain,  simple  precepts  whereby  health  may  be  secured. 
*  Our  preceding  remarks  on  diet,  cleanliness,  bathing,  &c.  in- 
clude almost  every  thing  of  importance  on  this  subject;  so  that 
what  follows  is,  properly  speaking,  only  a  recapitulation  of  the 
preceding  rules.     We  do  not  pretend   to  offer  remedies  for 


&79  D0>1BSTIC  COOKERY. 

every  disorder.  The  attempt  would  be  foolish  l^ld  pretump^ 
tuous ;  for  the  same  disorder  frequently  requires  a  different 
mode  of  treatment  according  to  the  age>  constitution^  and  other 
peculiarities  of  the  patient.  We  will^  however,  subjoin  a  few 
easy  directions  for  the  treatment  of  accidents  which  require 
immediate  aid,  t^nd  where  the  assistance  of  a  prqfessional  man 
cannot  be  obtained  in  proper  time. 

Moderation,  in  every  respect,  ought  to  be  the  first  and 
leading  maxim  of  those  who  wish  to  live  long  and  healkhjr. 
Both  extremes  in  the  most  opposite  things,  frequently  border 
on  each  other.  The  greatest  joy  may  occasion  the  most  sen* 
sible  pain ;  on  the  contrary,  moderate  pain  is  often  accompanied 
with  feelings  not  altogether  disagreeable.  The  highest  pleasure^ 
indeed,  is  closely  connected  with  aversion,  and  it  is  difficult  to 
avoid  the  latter  after  the  ei^oymcnt  of  the  former.  Hence 
prudence  enjoins  us,  to  opposp  the  progress  of  violent  sensa- 
tions and  affections,  before  they  have  attained  the  highest 
degree. 

Cleanliness  is  a  principal  duty  of  man,  and  an  unclean  or 
filthy  person  is  never  completely  healthy.  It  is  better  to  wash 
ourselves  ten  times  a  day,  than  to  allow  one  dirty  spot  to 
remain  on  the  skin.  On  a  place  where  impurities  are  suffered 
to  dog  the  pores,  not  only  insensible  perspiration,  but  likewise 
the  absorption  by  the  skin  is  entirely  suppressed ;  and  if  the 
whole  body  be,  as  it  were,  covered  with  a  varnish  formed  of 
perspired  matter,  it  is  impossible  that  a  person  in  such  a  statg 
can  possess  a  salubrious  blood,  or  enjoy  good  health. 

Many  diseases  originate  from  a  corrupted  atmosphere,  but 
a  still  greater  number  from  the  sudden  changes  of  the  otr. 
Hence  the  necessity  of  exposing  ourselves  daily  to  such 
changes,  and  of  renewing  the  air  in  the  house  and  apartments 
we  inhabit,  by  opening  the  doors  and  windows  every  clear 
morning,  or  during  the  day,  as  often  as  it  can  be  conveniently 
done.  Upon  the  whole,  to  encounter  cold  weather,  however 
intense,  has  the  effect  to  brace  the  fibres  of  the  system  in 
general,  and  is  attended  with  danger  only,  when  we  suddenly 
reinove  to  a  warmer  temperature.  For  tliis  reason,  it  is 
extremely  injudicious,  and  a  bad  compliment  paid  to  a  visitor, 
to  invite  him  to  the  fireside,  upon  his  first  entering  a  l^use  :— 
we  should  better  consult  his  healthy  by  x^onducUng  him  to  a 


FAHILT  PHYSICIAN.  671 

room,  or  to  some  distance  from  the  fire,  till  the  temperature 
of  his  body  be  more  approaching  that  of  the  apartment. 

Every  thing  calculated  to  remove  or  cure  diseases  may  also 
produce  them;  for,  whatever  has  a  tendency  to. accomplish 
useful  changes  in  the  body,  may,  under  different  and  opposite 
circumstances,  be  attended  with  the  contrary  effect*  Hence 
no  medidne  whatev^  ought  to  be  used  as  daily  food— a 
HiYoarite  practice  amongst  invalids  and  valetudinarians. 

WeaUy  individuals  ought  to  eat  frequently,  but  little  at  a 
tame :  the  number  of  meals  should  correbpond  with  the  want 
of  strength ;— rit  is  less  hurtful  to  a  debilitated  person,  to  eat 
a  few  mouthfuls  every  hour,  than  to  make  two  or  three  hearty 
meals  in  a  day. 

There  is  no  instance  on  record  of  any  person  having  injured 
Ins  health,  or  endangered  bis  life;  by  drinking  water  with  hia^ 
meals;  but  wine,  beer,  and  spiiats,  have  generated  a  much 
greats  number  and  diversity  of  patients, .  than  would  fill  all 
the  hospitals  in  the  world.  Such  are  (he  effects  of  intemperancei 
in  diet,  particularly  in  the  article  of  drink ;  for  neither  beer, 
wine,  nor  spirits,  are  of- themselves  hurtful,  if  used  with  mode- 
ration, and  in  a  proper  habit  of  body.-— It  is  a  vulgar  prejudice,, 
that  water  disagrees  with  many  constitutions,  and  does  not 
promote  digestion  as  well  as  wine,  beer,  or  spirit^;  on  thft 
contrary,  pure  water  is  greatly  preferable  to  all  brewed  ai^ 
distilled  liquors,  both  with  a  view  of  bracing  the  digestive 
organs,  and  preventing  complaints  which  arise  frmn  acrimony, 
QX  fubiess  pf  the  blood. 

It  is  an  observation  not  less  important  than  true,  that  by> 
attending  merely  to  a  proper  diet,  a  phlegmatic  habit  may 
frequently  be  changed  into  a  sanguine  one,  and  the  hypochon- 
driac may  be  so  far  converted  as  to  become  a  cheerful  and. 
contented  member  of  society. 

Witere  the  animal  functions  are  duly  performed,  the  secre* 
tions  gp  on  regularly ;  and  the  different  evacuations  so  exactly 
corr^pond  to  the  quantity  of  aliment  taken  in,  in  «  given  tiipetf

MODE  OF  TREATING  VARIOUS  ACCIOlHTS. 

Of  jBurnt • 

In  slight  burns  which  do  not  break  th^skin^  it  it  oistomarj 
to  hold  the  part  near  the  fire  for  a  competent  time^  to  mb  it 
with  salty  or  to  lay  a  compress  upon  it  dipped  in  brandj.  But 
when  the  bum  breaks  or  blisters  the  skin,  the  part  ought  to  be 
bathed  in  turpentine,  and  dressed  with  Tumer's.cerate. 

Of  Bruises, 

In  slight  bruiaes  it  will  be  sufficient  to  bathe  the  part  with 
warm  vinegar,  to  which  a  little  brandy  or  rum  may  be  added, 
and  to  keep  cloths  wet  with  this  mixture  constantly  applied  to 
it.  This  is  more  proper  than  rubbing  it  with  brandy,  spirits 
of  wine,  or  other  ardent  spirits,  which  are  oooraionly  used  in 
such  cases. — In  some  parts  of  the  coubtry  the  peasants  apply 
to  a  recent  bruise  a  cataplasm  of  fresh  oow-<lung;  with  very 
happy  eiFects. 

When  a  bruise  is  very  violent  the  consequences  may  be 
serious,  to  prevent  which,  surgical  aid  ought  to  be  procured  as 
soon  as  possible. 

Slight  Wounds. 

When  simple  wounds  bleed  much,  lint  dipped  m  vm^ar 
&r  spirits  of  turjpentine  may  be  pressed  upon  the  suHkce  Ibr  a 
f^w  minutes,  and  retained  by  a  moderatdy  tight  bandage ;  but 
if  the  blood  spirts  out  in  jets,  it  ^ows  that  an  arMy  is  wouttd^ 
ed,  and  it  must  be  held  very  firmly  until  a  aarjgeon  alrives. 
But  when  the  blood  seems  to  flow  equally  fh>m  every  part  of  a 
wound,  and  there  is  no  reason  therefore  to  suppose  that  any 
Considerable  vessel  is  wounded,  it  may  be  permitted  to  bleed 
while  the  dressings  are  preparii^.  The  edges  of  the  wound 
are  then  to  be  gently  pressed  together,  and  retained  by  straps 
of  sticking  plaster,  made  as  follows : 

Melt  three  ounces  of  dracyton  with  half  an  ouncie  oT  resin, 
and  when  cooled  to  about  ihe  thickness  of  treade  spread  it 
upon  a  piece  of  smooth  soft  linen.    This  may  remain  on  for 


J 


tbHt  or  four  days,  uhIms  the  sore  becomes  painAil,  or  the  mat- 
ter smelU  oflensive,  in  which  cose  the  straps  of  plaster  must  be 
taken  off^  the  parts  washed  clean  with  warm  water,  and  fresh 
slips  of  plaster  applied,  nicely  adjusted  to  keep  the  wound  to-» 
gether.  The  slips  must  be  laid  over  the  wound  crossways 
and  reach  several  inches  beyond  each  side  of  it,  in  order  to 
hold  the  parts  firmly  together.  By  keeping  the  limb  or  pait 
very  still,  abstaim'ng  fronp  strong  liquors,  taking  only  light 
mild  food,  and  keeping  the  bowels  open,  all  simple  wounds 
may  be  easily  healed  in  this  manner;  but  poultices,  greaay 
salves,  or  fiUiqg  the  Found  with  lint,  will  have  an  opposite 
fiffeot. 

Even  ragged  and  torn  wounds  may  be  drawn  together  an^ 
healed  by  sticking  plaster,  without  any  other  salves  or  medi- 
cines. 

A  broken  shin,  or  slight  ruffling  of  the  skin,  may  be  covered 
with  lint  dipped  in  equal  parts,  vinegar  and  brandy,  and  left 
io  stick  on  unless  the  place  inflames,  and  then  weak  goulard  is 
the  best  remedy. 

Common  cuts  may  be  kept  U>gcther  by  a  strip  of  the  above 
sticking  plaster,  or  with  only  a  piece  of  fine  linen  rag,  or  a 
thread  bound  round  them. 

The  rag  applied  next  to  a  cut,  or  wound  of  any  kind, 
should  be  always  of  white  linen ;  but  calico,  or  coloured  rags, 
will  do  quite  as  well  for  outward  bandages. 

Important  wounds  should  always  be  put  under  the  pare  of 
a  skilful  surgeon. 

Thortu,  Splinters,  S^c. 

To  run  prickles  or  thorns,  such  as  those  of  roses,  thistles, 
chesnuts,  &c.  or  little  splinters  of  wood,  bone,  &c.  into  the 
hands,  feet,  or  legs,  is  a  very  common  accident,  and  provided 
.any  such  substance  is  immediately  extracted,  is  seldom  attend- 
ed with  any  bad  consequences.  But  the  more  certainly  to  pre- 
vent any  such,  a  compress  of  linen  dipped  in  warm  water  may 
be  applied  to  the  part,  or  it  may  be  bathed  a  little  while  in 
warm  water. 

If  the  thorn  or  splinter  cannot  be  extracted  directly,  or  if 
any  part  of  it  be  left  in,  it  causes  an  inflammation>  and  nothing 
but  timely  precaution  will  prevent  its  coming  to  an  abscess. 


A  plaster  of  ehoemaker*8  wax  spread  upon  leather,  draws  these 
winds  remarkably  well.  When  it  is  known  that  any  part  of 
it  remains,  an  expert  surgeon  would  open  the  place  and  take  it 
out ;  but  if  it  is  unobserved,  as  will  sometimes  happen  when 
the  substance  is  very  small,  till  the  inflammation  b^fins,  and 
no  advice  can  be  at  once  procured,  the  steam  of  warm  water 
sliould  be  applied  to  it  at  first,  and  then  a  poultice  of  cramb  of 
bread  and  milk,  with  a  few  drops  of  Peruvian  balsam. 

It  is  absolutely  necessary  that  the  injured  part  should  be 
kept  in  the  easiest  posture,  and  as  still  as  possible. 

If  this  does  not  soon  succeed,  good  advice  must  be  applied 
to  without  delay,  as  an  accident  of  this  kind  neglected,  or  im- 
properly  treated,  may  be  the  occasion  of  losing  a  limb. 

In  this  and  all  cases  of  inflammfttioD>  a  forbearance  firom 
animal  food  and  fermented  liquors  is  always  advisable. 

Warts  and  Corns. 

Warts  may  be  safely  destroyed  by  tying  them  closely  reund 
the  bottom  with  a  silk  thread,  or  a  strong  flaxen  thread  waxed. 
Or  they  may  be  dried  away  by  some  moderately  corroding 
application,  such  as  the  milky  juice  of  fig  leaves,  of  clielidoai- 
um  (swallow  wort,)  or  of  spurge.  Warts  may  also  be  destroy- 
ed by  rubbing  them  with  the  inside  of  bean  shells.  But  these 
corrosives  can  only  be  procured  in  sumtner,  and  persona  who 
have  very  delicate  thin  skins  should  not  use  them,  as  they  may 
occasion  a  painful  swelling.  Instead  of  them  a  little  vinegar 
impregnated  with  as  much  salt  as  it  will  dissolve,  is  very 
proper.  A.  plaster  may  also  be  made  of  sal  ammoniac  and  some 
galbanum,  which  well  kneaded  together  and  applied,  seldom 
£uls  of  destroying  them. 

The  most  general  or  only  cause  of  corns  is  shoes  either  too 
bard  and  stiff,  or  too  small. 

The  cure  consists  in  softening  the  corns  by  repeated  washing 
and  soaking  the  feet  in  pretty  hot  water ;  then  cutting  the  com, 
when  softened,  with  a  sharp  penknife^  without  wounding  the 
flesh,  and  afterwards  applying  a  leaf  of  houseleek,  ground  ivy, 
or  purslain,  dipped  in  vinegar,  upon  the  place.  Or  instead  of 
these  leaves  they  may  be  dressed  every  day  with  a  plaster  of 
sipple  diacylon^  or  of  gum  ammoniacum  softened  in  vinegar.

The  increase  or  return  of  corns  can  only  be  prevented  by 
avoiding  the  cause  that  produces. them. 

Of  Disloeationt. 

Any  person  of  common  sense  and  resolution,  who  is  present 
'when  a  dislocation  happens,  may  often  be  of  more  service  to 
the  patient  than  the  most  expert  surgeon  can^  after  the  swelling 
and  inflammation  have  come  on.  When  these  are  present,  it 
is  difficult  to  know  the  state  of  the  joint,  and  dangerous  to 
attempt  a  reduction;  arid  by  waiting  till  they  are  gone  off,  the 
muscles  become  so  relaxed,  and  the  cavity  filled  up,  that  the 
bone  can  never  afterwards  be  retained  in  its  place. 

A  recent  dislocation  may  generally  be  reduced  by  extension 
alone,  which  must  always  be  greater  or  less,  according  to  the 
strength  of  the  muscles  which  move  the  joint,  the  age,  robust- 
ness, and  other  circumstances  of  the  patient.  All  that  is  neces- 
sary after  the  reduction  is  to  keep  the  part  easy,  and  to  apply 
to  it  cloths  dipped  in  vinegar,  or  camphorated  spirits  of  wine. 

Of  JBroken  Bones. 

We  would  advise  our  female  readers  always  to  recommend 
people  to  apply  to  an  expert  and  skilful  surgeon  when  such  an 
accident  occurs  ;  to  keep  the  patient  dry,  clean,  and  cool,  and 
to  raise  him  up  and  lay  him  down  with  great  gentleness. 

Of  Strains. 

Strains  are  often  attended  with  worse  consequences  than 
broken  bones.  The  reason  is  obvious ;  they  are  generally 
neglected.  Country  people  generally  immerse  a  strained  limb 
in  cold  water.  This  is  very  proper,  provided  it  be  done 
immediately,  and  not  kept  in  too  long.  But  the  custom  of 
keeping  the  part  immersed  in  cold  water  for  a  long  time,  is 
certainly  dangerous.  It  relaxes  instead  of  bracing  the  part« 
and  is  more  likely  to  produce  a  disease  than  remove  one. 

Wrapping  a  garter,  or  some  other  bandage,  pretty  tight 
about  the  strained  part,  is  likewise  of  use.  It  helps  to  restore 
the  proper  tone  of  the  vessels,  and  prevents  the  action  of  the 
parts  from  increasing  the  disease.  It  should  not,  however,  be 
applied  too  tight.    But  what  we  would  recommmd  above  aU

Of  Ruptwes. 

Children  are  most  liable  to  this  disease,  occasioned  by  ex- 
cessive crying,  coughing,  vomiting,  or  the  like*  On  the  Bnt 
appearance  of  a  rupture  in  an  infant,  it  ought  to  be  laid  ufioa 
its  back,  with  its  head  very  low.  While  in  this  posture,  if  tlie 
gut  does  not  return  of  itself,  it  may  easily  be  put  up  by  gentle 
pressure.  After  it  is  returned,  a  piece  of  sticking  pbu^er  aoaj 
be  applied  over  the  part,  and  a  proper  truss  or  bandage  must 
be  constantly  worn  for  a  xxmsiderable  time.  The  method  of 
making  and  applying  these  rupture  bandages  for  children  ia 
pretty  well  known.  The  child  must,  as  far  as  possible,  be 
kept  from  crying,  and  from  all  violent  motion,  till  the  rupture 
is  quite  healed. 

Of  Substances  stopped  between  the  Mouth  and  St^nnaeh. 

Though  accidents  of  this  kind  are  vary  common,  and 
extremely  dangerous,  yet  they  are  generally  the  effect  of 
carelessness.  Children  should  be  taught  to  chew  the  food 
well,  and  to  put  nothing  into  their  mouths  which  it  would  be 
dangerous  fbr  them  to  swallow.  But  children  are  not  the 
only  persons  guilty  of  this  piece  of  imprudence.  Many  adults 
pot  pins,  nails,  and  other  sharp-pointed  substances,  in  their 
mouths,  upon  every  occasion,  and  some  even  sleep  with  the 
former  there  all  night  This  conduct  is  exceedingly  incautious, 
as  a  fit  of  coughing,  or  twenty  odier  accidents,  may  force  over 
the  substance  before  the  person  be  aware. 

When  any  substance  is  detained  in  the  gullet,  there  are  two 
ways  of  removing  it,  via.  either  by  extracting  it,  or  pushing  it 
down.    The  safest  and  most  certain  way  is  always  to  extractor

nisy  be  poshed  down  without  danger  are,  all  common  noorish* 

ing  ones,  as  bread,  flesh,  fraits,  and  the  like*   All  indigestible 

bodies^  as  cork,  wood,  bones,  pieces  of  metal,  and  such  like, 

ought,  if  possible,  to  be  extracted,  especially  if  these  bodies  b# 

aharp-pointed,  as  pins,  needks,  fish  bones,  bits  of  glass,  &c. 

When  such  substances  have  not  passed  in  too  deep,  wa 

abould  endeaToor   to  extract   them  with  our  fingers,  which 

method  often  succeeds.      When   they  are  lower  we  should 

make  use  of  a  pair  of  nippers,  compasses,  or  of  a  pretty  strong 

ifon  frire»  bent  at  one  end,  and  introduced  in  the  flat  way. 

There  ahoiild  likewise  be  a  curve  at  the  end  it  is  held  by, 

which  should  alao  be  secured   by  a  string.     Flexible  rings 

made  of  wool,  thread,  ailk,  or  smaU  pack*thread,  waxed,  and 

tied  to  a  handle  of  whalebone  or  iron  wir^  will,  in  many  cases, 

be  found  of  considerate  advantage.      Where  tlie  substance 

has  stoppad  only  part  of  the  passage  of  the  gullet,  a  bit  of 

aponge  may  be  introduced,  after  which  a  few  drops  of  water, 

and  when  the  sponge  is  dilated  draw  it  up.     A  bit  of  tough 

meat  sometimes  answers  as  well,  and  is  much  safinr.     When  it 

is  proper  and  necessary  to  force  the  obstructing  body  down,  a 

piece  of  whalebone  or  wire  will  effect  this  purpose.      But  in 

every  such  case  the  assistance  of  a  8UTge<Hi  should  be  procured 

when  poesible. 

0/  drowned  PerMiu. 

Whenever  a  human  being  has  been  under  water,  and  is 
apparently  drowned,  we  should  always  endeavcmr  to  afibiti 
him  the  most  efectoal  relief,  and  never  to  give  ^im  up  as 
itrecove^ble  too  soon,  since  it  has  often  been  known,  that- 
nnitil  the  expiration  of  two  andsometimes  even  of  three  hoars, 
such  persims  have  exhibited  some  tokens  of  life. 

The  iirtention  which  should  be  pursued  is,  that  of  unloading 
the  hmgs  and  ^ain,  and  restoring  the  natural  warmth  and 
draitlaiitm,  &c.  Though  cold  was  by  no  means  the  caase  of 
tjie  pereon^s  death,  yet  it  will  prove  an  effectual  obstacle  to  his 
secovery.     For  this  xeaaen,  after  surippmg  him  rf  his  wet 


M4  BOM B8TI0  COOKBRT. 

clothes,  his  body  mnst  be  strongly  rubbed  for  a  considerable 
time  urith  ooarse  linen  doths,  as  wann  as  they  can  be  made; 
and  as  soon  as  a  well-heated  bed  can  be  got  ready,  he  may  be 
laid  in  it,  and  the  rubl>ing  should  be  continued.  Warm  ckiths 
ought  likewise  to  b^  frequently  applied  to  the  stomach  and 
bowels,  and  hot  bricks,  or  bottles  filled  with  warm  water,  to 
the  soles  of  his  feet.  Bleeding  in  th^  neck  will  also  be  of 
service* 

In  order  to  renew  the  breathing,  the  smoke  of  tobacco  may 
be  blown  into  the  lungs,  by  means  of  a  pipe  or  funnel.  The 
fume  of  tobacco  should  likewise  be  thrown  up  as  speedily  and 
plentifully  as  possible  into  the  intestines,  in  form  of  clyster. 

The  strongest  yolatiles  should  likewise  be  applied  to  the 
nose,  as  spirit  of  hartshorn,  sal  volatile  oleoium,  burnt  feathery 
and  such  like.  The  nose  may  likewise  be  tickled  with  a  leadier, 
and  the  powder  of  dried  marjoram,  tobacco,  or  rue,  blown  up 
the  nostrils.  The  temples  and  pit  of  the  stomach  may  be 
frequently  rubbed  with  warm  brandy  or  spirits  of  wine,  a 
few  drops  of  which  may  likewise  be  put  into  the  mouth  by 
means  of  a  feather.  Should  these  endeavours  prorct  unsuc- 
cessful, the  patient  may  be  put  into  a  warm  bath,  or  laid 
among  warm  ashe^  hot  dung,  sand,  or  such  like. 

As  soon  as  the  patient  discovers  any  motion,  he  may  take 
frequently  a  table-spoonful  of  the  oxymel  of  squills  diluted 
with  warm  water ;  or,  if  that  medicine  is  not  at  hand,  a  strong 
infusion  of  sage,  camomile  flowers,  ovcard»u$  henedietuSf 
sweetened  with  honey,  may  be  used  in  its  stead;  wh^re  no* 
thing  else  can  be  had,  some  warm  water,  with  the  addition  of 
a  little  common  salt,  should  be  given. 

We  are  by  no  means  to  discontinue  our  assistance  as  soon  as 
the  patients  discover  some  tokens  of  life,  since  they  somefcimQi 
expire  after  the  first  appearances  of  recovering.  The  waan 
and  stimulating  applications  are  still  to  be  continned,  and 
small  quantities  of  some  cordial  liquor  ought  frequently  to  be 
administered. 

The  durections  with  respect  to  persons  who  have  been 
strangled  are  so  nearly  the  same  with  those  lur  drowned 
people,  that  we  think  it  unnecessary  to  mention  them.  The 
general  intention  is  the  same,  via.  to  renew  the  eurcnlation, 
respiration,  &c,  which  must  b^  attempted  by  bleedings  Uowhig

warm  «ir  into  die  lungs  and  artettihes,  aiidl  applying  warm 
aabttanees^  as  ashes,  sait^  or  snch  Hke,  to  the  whole  surface  of 
thebody. 

Suck  peisons  as  hav«  the  misfortune  to-be  deprived  of  the

.  Of  namaus  yapamn* 

The  vapours  which  exhale  from  cfasfroeal;  and  from  wiiiey 
cider,  beer,  or  other  liquors  in  a  state  of-  ferraentatiGn,  contain 
something  poisonous  and  destructive  to  life.  The  vaponrr 
arising,  from  caves,  pits,  or  wells,  which  have  been  long  shut 
up,  are  also  dangerous.  -  When  any  person  has  exposed  him- 
self in  such  situations  as  to  lose  feeling  snd  understanding,  Ae 
fi^owing  means  must  be  used  for  their  recovery :  ' 

The  patient  should  be  exposed  to  a  very  pure,  firedi,  and 
open  air;  and  volatile  salts,  or  other  stimukting  substances, 
held  to  his  nose.  He  should  next  be  bled  in  the  arm,  or,  if 
that  does  not  succeed,  in  the  neck.  •  His  legs  ought  to  be  put 
into  warm  water,  and  well  rubbed.  As  soon  as  he  can  swallow, 
some  lemonade,  or  water  and  vinegar,  with  the  addition  of  a 
little  nitre,  may  be  given  him. 

Nor  are  sharp  clysters  by  any  means  to  be  neglected ;  these 
may  be  made  by  adding  to  the  common  clyster  syrup  of 
buckthoraand  tincture  of  senna,  of  each  two  ounces;  or,  in 
their  stead,  half  an  ounce  of  Venice  turpentine,  dissolved  in 
the  yolk  of  an  egg,  may  be  added.  Should  these  things  not 
be  at  hand,  two  or  three  laige  spoonfuls  of  common  salt  may 
be  put  into  the  clyster.  The  sam^  means,  if  necessary,  must 
be  used  to  keep  up  the  natural  warmth,  circulatioo^  &c.  as- 
were  recommended  in  the  former  article. 

Of  Intoxication, 

The  effects  of  intoxication  are  often  fatal.  As  it  is  often 
the  lot  of  wives,  daughters,  or  other  female  inmates,  to  assist 
relatives  in  this  dangerous  situation,  we  will  give  a  few  plain 
directions  for  their  conduct  in  this  affair. 

No  drunk  person  should  be  left  by  himself  till  his  dotJies 
have  been  loosened,  and  his  body  laid  in  such  a  posture  as  is

In  swoonings  which  arise  from  nervous  disorders,  or  indeed 
in  fainting  fits  of  any  kind,  firesh  air  is  alwvys  of  the  greatest 
importance.  Hence  it  islii^ly  improper  to  crowd  croaBd  the 
.patient  to  his  assistance.,    This  is  cruel  and  dabgeroiis.      * 

OF  THE  IMPROVEMENT  OF  AIR  IN  DWELLING-HOUSES. 

,  A  house  built  on  a  rising  gtound,  on  a  healthy  soil,  in  an 
open  dry  country,  and  neither  exposed  to  the  greatest  d^^iee 
of  cold  in  winter,  nor  to  the  highest  point  of  heat  in  Bammer  ; 
*-such  a  house  may  be  said  to  stand  in  a  healthy  sitnatioD. 
'Hence  those  apartments  are  the  most  healthful  as  well  as 
comfortable  to  the  individual,  which  enjoy  a  pure  and  free 
circulation  of  air  in  summer,  and  the  cheering  rays  of  the  sun 
in  winter :  the  heat  of  summer  being  considerably'  tempered 
by  the  former,  and  the  severity  of  a  cold  winter  much  diated 
by  the  latter.  Further,  a  proper  siae  and  height  is  requisite 
to  constitute  a  healthful  apartment ;  for  low  rooms  a^  detri- 
mental to  health,  particularly  when  they  are  inhabited  by  large 
families,  and  when  they  are  seldom  aired,  or  rather,  whidi  is 
frequently  the  case,  when  every  access  of  air  is  carefully  ex- 
cluded by  close  shutters,  curtains,  screens,  &c. 

The  better  to  judge  of  the  salubrity  of  the  air  in  any  district, 
we  should  examine  the  properties  of  the  wells  and  springs ;  for 
air  and  water  both  absorb  the  saline  and  mineral  particles  of 
the  soil.  We  may  pretty  certainly  conclude,  that  a  countiy 
producing  good  water  is  provided  likewise  with  a  salubrious 
aur.  As  the  best  water  is  tasteless,  so  the  purest  air  is  free 
from  any  smell  whatever. 

The  most  certain  marks,  by  which  to  distinguish  whether 
the  air  be  damp  or  not,  are  the  following  :-^the  widls  or  tapestiy 
change  their  colour ;  bread  in  closets  acquires  a  mouldy  sor&oe ; 
spunges  in  the  rooms  retain  their,  moisture ;  loaf  sugar  turns 
soft ;  iron  rusts ;  brass  and  copper  acquire  a  green  colour  or 
verdigrease ;  and  wooden  furniture  moulders  and  crumbles  to 
pieces. 

Every  room  is  filled  with  three  difierent  eiraia  of  air:  I. 
the  lower  part  of  the  room  contains  the  beavieat  species  of  air, 

/ 


J 


.  TXUlhi  PHYSICIAN.  506 

• 

namely^  fixed  or  carbonic  acid  gas,  particularly  in  rooms 
aitaated  on  the.  groand-floor,  or  eren  under-gnmnd ;  2.  the 
middle  part  ci  the  room  is  filled  with  the  lighter  atmospheric 
air;  and  S.  the  uppermost  stratum  contains  the  lightest  or 
■ff%"ipi^^*^^^  air,  the*  most  corrupted  of  the  three,  in  cons^ 
quence  of  the  processM  it  has  undergone  by  respiration  and 
combustion.  In  lofty  apartments  this  contaminated  species  of 
air  ia  not  inspired  by  the  lungs;  because  the  middle  stratum, 
or  the  most  wholesome  of  the  three,  extends  to  a  height  above 
thatoi  a  man. 

The  windows  and  doors  of  sitting  and  bed-rooms,  when  it 
can  be  done  oonventently,  ought  to  be  left  open  for  a  certain 
space  of.  time,  every  day.  This,  however,  requires  to  be  done 
at  the  proper  time,  neidier  too  early  in  the  morning,  nor  whetf 
it  grows  dark  in  the  evening,  during,  the  vernal  and  autumnal 
months ;  nor.  at  the  time  when  th^  horizon  i»  overspread  with 
a.  thick  fog.  The  windows  shouU  be  opened,  when  the  air  is 
pure  and .  serene ;  or  in  general,  when  there  is  less  danger  to 
be  appiehended  from  the  extiemal  air-  than  from  that  within. 
Sometimes  it  may  be  proper  to  nnke  use  of  what  is  called 
pumping  the  room,  or  moving  the  door  backward  and  forward 
for  some  minutes  together;  but  in  spring  and  autumn  our 
sitting-rooms,  and  even  in  winter  our  bed-rooms,  ought  to  btf 
thoroughly  perflated  every  dear  day,  by  currents  of  fresh  air, 
for  a  ccmsiderable  time. 

In  the  hot  days  of  summer,  the  windows  may  be  opened 
early  in  the  morning  and  in  the  evening,  in  order  to  oool  and 
to  refresh  the  heated  air  of  the  room  by  that  f^om  without 
It  is,  however,  not  safe  (and  has  sometimes  pioved  fiital)  to 
leave  the  windows  of  a  bed*room  open  at  night  during  the 
summer  months,  as  there  is  no  sinall  hazard  of  checking 
perspiration  by  the  cool  night  air*;  the  susceptibility  of  the 
pores  being  then  very  much  increased  by  the  heat  of  the  day, 
and  the  warmth  of  lihelE>ed.  Booms  which  we  inhabit  in  the 
day-time  may  be  safely  left  (fien  during  the  night. — In  sum« 
mer-houses,  or  such  as  are  surrounded  with  plants  and  trees;; 
it  will  be  proper  not  to  open  the  windows  of  bed  or  other 
WxiOB  till  scBie  time  after  sun^rise,  and  to  shut  them  at  sun- 
set: they  require  also  to.be.  opened  later  and  shut  sooner,  when 
it  is  haay,  tfaa&  in  serene  weather.

ON  AIR  AND  WEATHER. 

Damp  or  moist  air  suddenly  relaxes  and  debilitetes;  it 
occasions  a  slowness  in  the  ciicuktion  of  tile  fluids,  which 
gives  rise  to  stagnations ;  it  impedes  both  the  circulation  o( 
blood  and  the  secretion  of  homonnr,  by  checking  the  insensible 
perspiration.  If  the  moisture  of  the  air  increases,  we  eEperi- 
ence  aik  unacoountable  torpor  and  eimiM;  with  the  loss  of 
energy  we  lose  our  gaiety,  and  the  mind  is  depressed  with  the 
body.  Moisture,  by  diminishing  perspirationi  produoas  dis* 
orders  of  the  throat,  the  breast,  and  the  abdomen* 

Dr^  and  cool  air,  from  its  possessing  a  due  dcyreo  of 
elasticity,  promotes  in  an  extraordinary  degree  the  serenity 
and  alertness  of  the  mind  and  body.  But  a  dry  and  very 
cold  air  generates  inflammatory  diseases.  Diy  and  hot  air 
affecte  us  like  heal,  and  enervates  the  body.  Bat  a  dry  airji 
which  is  not  too  warm,  is  both  agreeable  and  healthy. 

Great  and  sudden  changes  from  a  warm  to  a  ooldi  or  from 
a  light  to  a  heavy  air,  are  highly  injurious  ta  valetudinariaiia 
as  well  as  to  the  healthy.  A  moden^tely  heavy  and  elastic  air 
is  the  most  pleasant  and  salutary  to  the  human  body. 

Among  the  different  tctnds  the  long  continued  north  utind 
is  comparatively  the  most  wholesome.  The  south  wind  weakeot 
and  relaxes'the  body.    The  morning  wind  is  very  drying ;  but 


692  OOMBBTIC  CdOKBRY. 

evening  winds  are  cool  and  moiirt^  being  genentiy  abcompiaiied 
with  rain  and  changeable  weather. 

Of  the.foiir  9e4i$&M  of  the  yeiur,  the  auitann  is  the  most 
unhealthy.  Too  light  a  dna^  and  too  diin  stockings,  are  not 
adviaeable  at  this  season.  The  tjfiting  seoion  is,  in  general^ 
the  safest  and  most  hesikby.  Spring,  and  the  beginning  of 
$itmmer,'  are  most  salutary  to  children  and  young  persons; 
while  the  summer,  and  the  beginning  of  tficliiiMt/  agree  best 
with  theiqied.  The  latter  end  of.  autumn,  and  the  amcoeeding 
winter,  are  commonly  the  most  healthy  seasons  to  persons  of  a 
middle  age. 


ADVICE 

,  •  .    .      .     , 

TO 

FEMALE  SERVANTS 


Observations  adapted  to  Femak  Servants  general^. 

'Ji.  HIS  advice  is  addressed  to  female  servants  in  general,  as 
it  is  calculated  to  promote  the  utility  and  happiness  of  servants 
in  every  station.  Even  mistresses  may  find  in  it  some  useful 
hints  not  unworthy  of  their  attention. 

One  of  the  greatest  and  most  advantageous  qualifications  in 
all  servants  (but  particularly  females)  is  that  of  preserving  a 
good  temper,  and  endeavouring  to  the  utmost  of  their  abilities 
to  give  universal  satisfaction.     Possessed  with  a  strong  desire  ot 
pleasing,  you  will  seldom  fail  of  doing  it.     A  corresponding 
good  temper  will  be  charmed  with  your  readiness,  and  a  bad 
one  disarmed  of  great  [part  of  its  harshness ;  and  though  you 
may  be  somewhat  deficient  at  first  in  executing  the  business  in 
which  you  are  employed,  yet  when  they  see  it  is  not  occasion- 
ed by  obstinacy  or  indolence,  they  wiU  rather  instruct  you  in 
what  they  find  you  ignorant,  tfum  be  angry  that  you  are  so. 


JkDTieE  TO  F9MALB  SntTANTS.  €M 

On  Jbe  contrary,  though,  yon  iaay  diidmrge  your  busineM  with 
die  greateit  propriety,  yet,  if  yqo  iqppear  carelewor  kidkFerent 
whether  31011  pleaae  or  not,  your  Mrvkps  will  l^Me  grMt  part  of 
Ihehr  merit  If  you  are  fearibl  of  offendingi  you  can  ecaroely 
ofiend  at  all  ^  beeause  that  very  fearfulneas  la  an  indication  of 
your  retpect  ier  those  you  serve,  and  intinatee  a  denre  ef 
deservinf  their  approbation.  In  short,  a  -good  temper  ia  the 
moBt  Valuable  of  female  qualifications,  and  will  inftiUibly  con« 
duct  its  podtessors,  with  ease  and  tranquillity,- through  every 
stag»of  life. 

Be  carelul  to  avoid  talebearing,  for  ikBt  is  a  vice  of  the 
most  pernicious  nature,  and  generally,  in  the  end,  turns  to  the 
disadvantage  of  those  who  practise  it  Many  things,  if  heard 
from  the  month*  that  first  speaks  theni,  would  be  wholly  inof* 
fiensive ; .  bujt  they  carry  a  different  meaning  when  repeated  by 
another.  Those  who  cannot  help  telling  all  they  hear,  are 
very  apt  (at  least  are  supposed  by  those  who  know  them)  to 
tdl  more  than  they  hear.  Nether  ought  you  to  interfere  with 
what  is  not  prc^rly  your  province ;  do  your  duty,  and  leave 
ethers  to  take  cure  of  theirs :  by  this  means  you  will  preserve 
peace,  and  acquire  the  love  of  all  your  ielloW'Servants,  without 
running  any  danger  of  disobliging  your  master  and  mistress, 
who,  however  they  may  appear  to  countenance  die  tales  you 
bring,  will  not,  in  their  hearts,  approve  of  your  Conduct 

Let  an  attachment  to  the  words  of  trudi  be  ever  impressed 
on  your  minds.  If  at  any  time  you  are  accused  of  a  fault 
which  you  are  eonsdous  of  having  committed,  never  attempt 
to  screen  it  with  a  falsehood  :  for  the  last  fault  is  an  addition 
to  the  former,  and  renders  It  more  inexcusable.  To  acknow 
ledg^  you  have  been  to  blame  is  the  surest  way  both  to  merit 
and  obtain  fiirgiveness ;  and  it  will  establish  an  opinion  that 
you  will  be  careful  to  avoid  the  like  trespass  for  the  future. 

Humility  and  a  modest  deportment  should  be  also  observed, 
as  they  are  not  only  becoming,  but  useful  qualifications  in  all 
servants.  If  your  mistress  should  be  angry  with  you  (even 
without  a  cause)  never  pretend  to  argue  die  case  with  her; 
but  give  he^ a  'soft  answer,*  for  that,  as  Solomon  says,  *puts 
away  wradi.'  If  she  is  a  discreet  woman  she  will  reflect,  after 
her  passion  is  over,  and  use  you  the  more  kindly ;  whereas  if 
you  endeavour  to  defend  yourself  by  sharp  and  pert  replies,  it 
22  4o 


5M..  '  SOJHESTIC  CQOKEEY. 

n^ill  give  her  a  real  occaaon  of  ofence,  jiutify  her  31  httmomv 
and  make  her  mdre  severelj  resent  the  like  in  futare. 

Above  all   things,   preserve  a  strict  atteDti<»i  to  hones^. 
Let  no  temptation  whatever  prevail  on  you  to  part  with  tliit 
inestimable  jewel.    To  cheat  or  defiraud  any  one  is  base  and 
wicked;'  but,  where  breach  of  trust  is  added,  the  crime  is 
infinitely  inareased.      ft  has  been  a  maxim  with  many,    to 
suppose  themselves  entitled   to  what  is  generally  called  tlie 
market  penny ;  but  this  is  an  ill-judged  an4  dishonest  notion* 
To  purloin  or  secret  any  part  of  what  is  put  into  your  hands, 
in  order  to  be  lain  out  to  the  best  advantage,  is  as  evident  a 
theft  as  if  yo)i  took  the  money  out  of  the  pockets  of  those  who 
intrust  you ;  and  in  doing  this  you  are  guiky  of  a  douUe 
wrong,  first,  to  your  master  or  mistress  who  sends  you  to 
market,  by  making  them  pay  more  than  they  ought ;  and  to 
the  tradesman  from  whom  you  buy,  by  making  him .  spgetat 
as  guilty  ot  imposition  in  exacting  a  greater  price  than  the 
coomipdity  is  worth.    Imagine  not,  that,  by  taking  pains  to 
find  out  where  you  can,  buy  cheapest,  you  are  entitled  to  the 
overplus  you  must  have  given  in  another  place ;  for  this  b  no 
more  than  your  duty,  and  the  time  it  takes  to  search  oat  the 
best  bargains  is  the  property  of  those  in  whose  service  you  are 
engaged.     To  obtain  the  character  of  a  good  market-woman 
is  certainly  a  valuable  acquisition,  and  far  superior  to  those 
pitiful  advantages,  which  cannot  be  continued  k>ng  without  a 
disgraceful  discovery.     You  can  live  with  very  few  who  will 
not  examine  into  the  market  prices ;  they  will  inquire  of  those 
who,  buy  for  themselves ;  and  as  some  people  have  a  foolidi 
way  of  boasting  of  the  bargains  they  make,  those  who  pretend 
to  buy  the  cheapest  wOl  be  the  most  readily  believed;  so  that, 
do  the  best  you  can,  you  will  be  able  to  give  but  very  indifteiw 
ent  satisfaction.     Buy,  therefore,  for  your  master  or  mistress 
as  you  would  for  {yourself ;  and  whatey|r.  money  remains, 
immediately  on  your  return  deliver  it  to  the  owner. 

Be  not  generous  at  the  expence  of  your  master  aiid  nustres^s 
property,  and  your  own  honesty.  Give .  not  any  thing  away 
without  thieir  consent.  Whai  you  find  there  is  any  thing  to 
spare,  and  that  it  is  in  danger  of  being  qwiled  if  «kept  longer, 
it  is  commendable  in  you  to  ask  leave  .to  dispose  .of  it  ^i^le  fit 
for  use.    If  such  permission  is  refiised,  yon  have  nothing  te 


ADVICS  TO  FBHALE  SERVANTS.  596 

•aswflr  im  on  that  aooonnt;  but  yoa  must  not  give  away  the 
least  morsel  without  the  approbation  of  those  to  whom  it 
belongs.  Be  careful  also  not  to  make  any  waste,  for  that  is  a 
crime  of  a  much  deeper  die  than  is  imagined  by  those  who  are 
guilty  of  it ;  and  seldom  goes  without  its  punishment,  by  th^ 
severe  want  of  that  which  they  have  so  lavishly  destroyed. 

Never  speak  in  a  disrespectful  'manner  of  your  master  or 
mistress,  nor  listen  to  any  idle  stories  related  by  others  to  their 
prejudice.  Always  vindicate  their  reputations  from  any  open 
aspersions  or  malicious  insinuations.  Mention  not  their  names 
in  a  familiar  manner  yourself,  nor  suffer  others  to  speak  of 
them  with  contempt.  As  far  as  you  can^  magnify  their  virtues; 
and  what  failings  they  may  have,  shadow  them  over  as  much 
as  possible.  When  this  is  known,  it  will  not  only  endear  you 
to  them,  but  also  gain  the  esteem  of  those  who  hear  you 
talk ;  for  though  many  people  have  the  ill  nature  to  be  pleased 
with  picking  out  what  they  can  to  the  prejudice  of  their  neigh* 
hours,  yet  none  in  their  hearts  approve  of  the  person  who 
makes  the  r^ort.  It  is  natural,  at  the  same  time  we  love  the 
treason,  to  hate  the  traitor. 

Avoid,  as  much  as  possible,  entering  into  any  dispute  or 
quarrels  with  your  fellow-servants.  Let  not  every  trifle  ruffle 
you,  or  occasion  you .  to  treat  them  with  grating  reflections, 
even  though  they  should  be  the  first  aggressors.  It  is  better 
to  put  up  with. a  small  affront,  than  by  returning  it,  provoke 
yet.  more,  and  raise  a  disturbance  in  the  family.  When 
quarrels  in  the  kitchen  are  loud  enough  to  be  heard  in  the 
parlour,  both  parties  are  blamed,  and  it  is  not  always  that  the 
innocent  person  finds  the  most  protection.  . 

If  you  live  in  a  considerable  famUy,  where  there  are  many 
men«servants,  you  must  be  very  circumspect  in  your  behaviour 
to  them.  As  they  have  in  general  little  to  do,  they  are  for  the 
most  part  saucy  and  pert  where  they  dare,  and  are  apt  to  take 
liberties  on  the  least  encouragement.  You  must  therefore 
carry  yourself  at  a  distance  towards  them,  though  not  with 
a  proud  or  prudish  air.  You  must  neither  look  as  if  you 
thought  yourself  above  them,  nor  seem  as  if  you  imagined 
every  word  they  spoke  intended  as  a  design  upon  you.  No : 
the  one  would  make  them  hate  and  affront  you ;  and  the  other 
would  be  turned  into  ridicule.    On   the  contrary,  you  must 


609  ]>OMESTiC  COOKBRY. 

behave  with  a  civility,  mixed  with  serieafiness;  but  on  ne 
account  whatever  suffer  your  civility  to  adimt  of  too  great 
Similiarities. 

If  yptt  live  in  a  tradesBMBTa  fiduily,  wh«r^  thare  are  appren* 
iices^  your  conduct  to  than  muat  be  of  a  differebt  nature. 
If  there  be  more  thali  one,  the- oldest  must  be  treated  with 
the  most  respect ;  but  at  the  same  time  you  must  not  bdbave 
to  the  others  in  a  haughty  or  imperious  manner.  You  most 
remember  that  they  are  servants  oidy  to  become  ntasten, 
and  should  therefore  be  treated  not  only  with  civility  bat 
kindness.  It  may  in  time  be  in  their  powor  to  recompence 
any  little  favoiir  you  do  them^  such  aa  mending  their  linen, 
or  other  offioes  of  that  kind^  when  you  have  a  leisure  hour; 
but  this  good*natute  must  not  proceed  too  fiff  as  they  advenes 
in  years^  lest  the  vanity  of  youth  diouM  make  them  imagine 
you  have  other  motives  for  it,  to  prevent  which,  yoit  mnil 
behave  with  an  open  civiMty  intermixed  with  a  modest  sn^ 
serious  reserve. 

Whenever  you  have  an  opportunity  attend  public  wofBliip^ 
and  spend  not  the  sabbath-day  in  mer^  idle  gossipiog  simI 
wandering  about;  or  is  loose  talk  and  behavlottr.  Yet  yoa 
must  not,  under  pretence  of  keefung  this  day  holy,  refuse  to 
do  any  necessisry  work ;  such  as  making  fires  and  beds,  dnm- 
ing  victuals  for  the  family;  milking  cows;  feeding  csltl^  or 
any  work  of  necessity  or  mercy.  '  TIse  sabbath  was  made  for 
dMu,*  says  our  Saviour,  'not  man  for  die  sabbath.'  ItwsB 
made  to  do  g^ood  to  men ;  not  lio  afflict  or  punish  theou  b^ 
deprive  them  of  any  real  comfort.  But  above  ail  neglect  not 
private  prayer,  and  read  the  Bibl^  diligently.

INSTRUCTIONS  TO  HOUSEKEEPERS. 

K  HE  bosineas  of  the  housekeeper  u  of  great  inportanoe,  9$ 
shehas^  in  a  manner,  all  the  hpuaehold  affairs  to  superintend,  the 
maid-servants  to  look  after^  and  to  direct  in  their  proper  busi- 
ness. However  virtuous  some  young  people  may  seem  in  the 
discharge  of  their  duty,  yet  certainly  it  ia  most  prudent  to 
commit  the  care  of  a  house  to  a  woman  of  age  and  experience, 
well  acquainted  with  the  worlds  and  who  has  either  kept  house 
herself^  or  been  l<Mig  in  the  service  of  others.  If  such  a  one 
be  a  grave,  sober,  virtuous  person,  she  is  the  most  fit  to  be 
nuule  choice  of;  and  whatever  be  the  character  of  her  mistress, 
she  will  gain  her  approbation  at  the  last.  The  servants  will 
treat  her  with  more  respect  than  they  would  a  giddy,  thought* 
less  person  ;  and  the  more  assiduous  she  is,  and  faithful  in  the 
trust  committed  to  her^  the  more  sure  she  will  be  of  a  pkee  on 
any  future  occasion. 

In  our  observations  on  domestic  management  we  have  treat- 
ed copiously  on  the  different  duties  of  housekeepers.  Any 
thing  further  on  this  subject  is  therefore  rendered  unnecesswy. 


INSTRUCTIONS  TO  THE  LADY'S  MAID. 

No  one  ought  to  pretend  to  be  properly  qualified  to  fill  the 
office  of  lady's  woman,  unless  her  education  has  been  tolerably 
good ;  for  being  obliged  to  be  neair  her  lady,  tJie  is  often  re- 
quired to  read,  and  to  do  some  fine  piecea  of  needle-work. 
It  is  her  duty  to  study  her  lady's  temper,  to  answer  civilly, 
and  always  to  evince  the  most  ready  compliance  with  the  orders 
Ae  receives ;  she  ought  also  on  all  occasions  to  defend  her 
lady^s  character,  to  avoid  repeating  what  may  have  been  com- 
nlunicated  in  a  moment  of  frankness,  and  not  to  do'  any  thing 
that  has  die  least  appearance  of  countenancing  an  intrigue. 

The  ptoper  tnellibd|  of  perfortning  the  general  business  of 
her  stiition^  may  be  mt^uli^d  by  attending  to  the  following 


68S  DOMESTIC  COOKBRT. 

approved  methods  of  washing  laces,  masUns,  gauzes,  owb- 
brics,  also  of  cleaning  gold  apd  silver  laces,  stuffs,  &c.  whidi 
articles  are  usually  under  the  lady-maid's  care. 

It  is  often  necessary  that  a  lady's  maid  should  possess  some 
knowledge  of  hair  dressing,  which  may  be  easily  acquired  by 
a  little  experience,  taste,  and  observation.  But  in  all  cases  an 
acquaintance  with  the  art  of  perfumery  is  desirable  ;  we  have 
therefore  annexed  some  approved  receipts,  which  will  be  found 
to  contain  almost  every  thing  of  value  on  this  subject. 

To  wash  Lace. 

Take  your  lace  and  soap  it  well  with  soft  soap,  after  which 
take  a  piece  of  plain  deal  board,  sew  a  piece  of  doth  on  it  very 
tight,  an^roU  your  lace  very  smooth  round  it  After  this  sew 
another  piece  of  doth  over  it,  and  put  it  into  a  clean  b(Mler  of 
water,  and  set  it  on  the  fire  till  the  water  is  scalding  hot ;  then 
shake  out  the  lace,  put  it  into  a  pan,  and  pour  the  water  on 
it.  MThen  you  have  done  this,  rest  one  end  of  the  board  on 
the  dresser  or  table,  and  with  the  other  rub  it  well  with  a  hard 
brush,  dipping  it  at  the  same  time  into  water,  and  pressing 
your  hand  downwards  with  the  brush  to  squeeze  out  the  soap 
and  dirt.  You  must  repeat  this  in  a  second  kettle  of  water, 
pressing  it  with  the  brush  as  before;  and  when  you  have  got 
the  dirt  all  out,  take  some  clean  water,  put  some  blue  into  it, 
and  let  it  boil  well :  after  which  make  some  good  starch,  give 
the  lace  a  gentle  boil  in  it,  and  then  squeeze  it .  well  oat. 
When  you  have  done  this,  hang  the  board  up  till  the  laoe  be 
thoroughly  dry,  and  then  take  off  the  cloth.  Then  pat  the 
laoe  between  some  clean  sheets  of  paper,  and  lay  a  heavy 
weight  on  it  all  night.  Take  off  the  weight  in  the  momiDg, 
and  your  lace  will  look  as  well  as  when  it  was  new. 

To  wash  Blond  Lace,  Muslin,  or  Gauze. 

The  same  method  for  one  of  these  will  do  for  the  whole. 
They  must  be  washed  in  three  different  waters,  each  of  which 
must  be  tolerably  warm  and  well  lathered.  When  yoa  have 
done  this,  rinse  them  wdl  in  good  blue  water,  then  hang  them 
up,  and  when  they  are  dry  let  them  be  well  stardied  and  hoiig 
up  sgain.  After  this  is  done,  put  half  an  ounce  of  yfinglaw 
into  three  pints  of  water/ and  let  it  boil  till  it  be  reduced  to 


ADYICB  TO  FBMALE  8BRTANTS.  ,       690 

one ;  then  dip  the  lace  or  gau2e  into  it,  squeeze  it  out  well^ 
and  then  iron  it.     Remember  that  the  starch  you  use  be  made 
thoroughly  stiff,  and  let  it  be  well  blued.     The  sooner  you 
iron  them  after  wiaishing  the  better,  more  espedally  the  gauzes. 
You  must  also  observe,  that  after  your  muslins  have  been 
boiled  in  two  lathers,  you  must  then  beat  up  a  third  very 
strong  and  well  blued ;  then  wash  them  out,  rinse  and  starch 
as  above,  hang  them  up  to  dry,  and  then  iron  th^.    They 
must  not  be  squeezed,   but  patted  between  the  hands :  lace 
will  always  look  best  by  being  ironed  on  the  wrong  side.     It 
will  not  be  amiss  if,  after  you  have  starched  your  muslins  and 
lace  very  stiff,  and  they  are  quite  dry,  you  throw  them  into  a 
little  cold  pump  water,  then  squeeze  them  well  out,  clap  them, 
wring  them  well,  and  iron  them.     This  will  help  to  make  them 
look  much  clearer,  and  little  inferior  to  new. 

To  wa$h  Cmnbries. 

Let  them  be  well  soaped,  and  then  washed  in  water  pretty 
warm ;  then  repeat  the  soapbg,  and  wash  them  with  the  water 
quite  hot.  When  you  have  done  this,  mix  some  soap  and 
blue  together,  rub  it  on  the  clothes,  lay  them  in  a  tub  or 
triller,  and  pour  some  boiling  hot  water  on  them.  Let  them 
lay  in  this  situation  for  about  two  hours,  then  wash  them  well 
out,  and  rinse  them  in  pump  water  well  blued.  When  you 
iron  them  be  careful  to  do  it  the  right  way,  as  you  will  other- 
wise be  subject  either,  to  singe  or  fray  them. — It  is  necessary 
to  observe,  that  whenever  you  boil  any  small  things,  you  first 
mix  your  soap  and  Uue  well  together,  and  then  pour  it  into 
the  water  to  boil  the  clothes.  This  will  keep  the  blue  from 
settling,  and  makes  die  clothes  perfecdy  dean  and  white. 

To  ckan  Gold  or  Siher  Lace,  Stuffs,  tfc. 

Take  a  three-penny  stale  loaf,  rub  the  crumb  of  it  well  be« 
tween  your  hands  till  it  is  quite  fine,  then  put  about  a  quarter 
of  an  ounce  of  powder  blue  to  it,  and  mix  the  whole  well 
together;  lay  it  plentifully  on  the  gold  and  silver,  and  rub  it 
wrell  with  your  hands,  and  it  will  soon  become  bright.  When 
this  is  done,  take  a  piece  of  dean  flannel  and  dust  the  crumbs 
well  off;  then  take  a  piece  of  crimson  velvet,  rub  it  gently, 
and  it  will  look  as  wdl  as  when  new. 


T0  preierve  Siltfer  and  Gold  from  Tarnishing. 

First  of  all  observe  never  to  put  gold  or  silver  into  a  box 
that  is  made  of  deal,  as  that  wood  is  very  prejudicial  to  it. 
After  at  is  used  fold  it  up  in  fine  India  paper,  over  which 
wrap  9ome  fine  wfaited  brown  paper  thoroughly  dry ;  th^n  fold 
the  whole  in  a  piece  of  green  baoze  well  aired,  and  put  them  in 
your  trunk,  in  whidi  you  should  always  keep  some  paper  well 
stained  with  saffron.

Hard  Pomatum, 

Prepare  equal  quantities  of  beeif  marrow  and  mutton  suet  as

.  Pot  Pourri, 

Put  into  a  large  China  jar  the  following  ingredients  in  layers, 
with  bay-salt  strewed  between  the  layers;  two  pecks  of  damask 
roses,  part  in  buds  and  part  blown ;  violets,  orange  flowers, 
and  jasmine,  a  handful  of  each  ;  orris-root  sliced,  benjamin  and 
atorax  two  ounces  of  each ;  a  quarter  of  an  ounce  of  musk ; 
a  quarter  of  a  poiind  of  angelica-root  sliced;  a  quart  of  the  red 
parts  of  clove-gillifiowers  ;  two  handfuls  of  lavender  flowers  ; 
half  a  handful  of  rosemary  flowers ;  bay  and  laurel  leaves,  half 
a  handful  of  each  ;  three  Seville  oranges,  stuck  as  full  of  cloves 
as  possible,  dried  in  a  cool  oven,  and  pounded ;  half  a  hand- 
ful of  knotted  marjoram;  and  two  handfuls  of  balm  of  Gitead 
dried.  Cover  all  quite  close.  When  the  pot  is  uncovered,  the 
perfume  is  very  fine. 

Poite  for  chopped  Hands,  and  which  will  preserve  them  smooth 

by  constant  Use. 

Mix  a  quarter  of  a  pound  of  unsalted  hog's  lard,  which  has 
been  washed  in  common'  aiid  then  rose  water,  with  the  yolks 
of  two  new-laid  eggs,  and  a  large  spoonful  of  honey.  *  Add  as 
much  fine  oatmeal,  or  almond  paste,  as  will  work  into  a  paste. 

For  chopped  Laps, 

Put  a  quarter  of  an  ounce  of  gum-benjamin,  storax,  and

apple  chopped,  a  bunch  of  black  grapes  bruised,  a  quarter  of 
a  pound  of  unsaltedt^butter,  and  two  ounces  of  bees'  wax,  into 
a  new  tin  sauce-pan.  Simmer  gently  till  the  wax,  &c.  are
