Hungary  Water. 

To  one  pint  of  highly  rectified  sjfurits  of  wine,  put  an  oooee 
of  oil  of  rosemary,  and  two,  drachms  of  essence  of  ambergrease; 
ahake'  the  bottle  well  several  times,  then  let  the  cork  remain 
out  twentyffour  hours.  After  a  month,  dmring  which  time 
shake  it  daily,  put  the  water  into  small  bottles. 

'  Hon^  Water. 

Take  a  pint  of  spirit  as  above,  and  three  dradiinsof  essetice 
of  ambergrease;  shake  them  well  daily. 

Lavender  Water. 

Take  a  pint  o£  spirit  as  above,  essential  oil  of  lavoider  one 
ounce,  essence  of  ambergrease  two  drachms ;  put  all  into  a  quart 
bottle,  and  shake  it  extremely  well. 

Essence  of  Flowers. 

Sekct  a  quantity  of  the  petals  of  any  flowers  whidi  have  an 
agreeable  fri^rance,  lay  them  in  an  earthen  vessel,  and  sprinkle 
a  little  fine  salt  upon  them :  then  dip  some  cotton  into  the  best 
Florence  oil,  and  lay  it  thin  upon  the  flowers,  continuing  a 
layer  of  flowers  and  a  layer  of  cotton  till  the  vessel  is  full.  It 
is  then  to  be  cleansed  down  with  a  bladder,  and  exposed  to  the 
heat  of  the  sun ;  in  about  a  fortnight  a  fragrant  oil  may  be 
squeezed  away  from  the  whole  mass,  which  will  yield  a  rich 
perfume. 

&sence  of  Lavender. 

Take  the  blossoms  from  the  stalks  in  warm  weather,  and 
qpread  them  in  the  shade  for  twenty-four  hours  on  a  linen 
doth,  then  bruise  and  put  them  into  warm  water,  and  leave 
them  closely  covered  in  a  still  four  or  five  hours  near  the 
fire.  After  this,  the  blossoms  may  be  distilled  in  the  usual 
way. 


ADYiOE  t6  Female  seayants.  'OW 

Milk  of  Roies. 

Mix  an  ounce  of  oil  of  almonds  with  a  pint  of  rose  water; 
and  then  add  ten  drops  of  the  oil  of  tartar. 

Rose  Water, 

When  the  roses  are  full  blown^  pick  off  the  leaves  carefully, 
and  allow  a  peck  of  them  to  a  quart  of  water.  Put  them  into 
a  cold  still  over  a  slow  fire,  and  distil  it  very  gradually :  bottle 
the  water,  and  cork  it  up  in  two  or  three  days. 

Wash. 

An  infusion  of  horse-radish  in  milk  makes  one  of  the  atfesi 
and  best  washes  for  the  skin ;  or  the  fresh  juice  of  house-leek, 
mixed  with  an  equal  quantity  of  new  milk  or  cream.  Honey 
water  made  rather  thick,  so  as  to  form  a  kind  of  varnish  on 
the  tkin,  is  a  useful  application  in  frosty  weather,  when  the 
skin  is  liable  to  be  chipped ;  and  if  it  occasions  any  irritsition 
or  uneasiness,  a  little  fine  flour  or  pure  hair  powder  should  be 
dusted'  on  the  hands  or  face.  •  A  more  elegant  wash  may  be^ 
made  of  four  ounces  of  potash,  four  of  rose  water,  two  ounces 
of  brandy,  and  two  of  lemon-juice,  mixed  in  two  quarts  of 
water.  A  spoonful  or  two  of  this  mixture  put  into'the  basin^ 
will  scent  and  soften  the  waters  intended  to  be  used. 

•  •  • 

An  excellent  Water  to  prevent  Hair  from  faUing  off,  and  to 

thicken  it.  "   *  '. 

Put  four  pounds  of  unadulterated  honey  into  a  still,  witB 
twelve  handfuls  of  the  tendrils  of  vines,  and  the  same  quantity 
df  roseiAary-tops.  Distil  as  cool  and  slowly  as  possible.  Th^ 
liquor  may  be  allowed  to  drop  till  it  begins  to  taste  sour. 


INSTRUCTIONS  TO  CHAMBERMAIDS. 

X  HE  chambermaid's  first  consideration  must  be  to  attend 
properly  to  the  oare  and  management  of  her  mistresses  clothes; 
for  as  it  is  alwa^^s  uncertain  at  what  time  she  may  want  tfaiem, 
so  it  is  essentially  necessary  they  should  be  ever  in  readiness.' 


uieiT  proper  pioctn ;  190  uuu,  11  you  bhuuiu  <je  luuuiy  -~"" 
upon  for  either.  It  wUl  be  more  convenient  to  you,  and  more 
pleasing  to  your  miatiess.  Be  particularly  careful  to  preserve 
the  linen  clean  and  nice,  and  be  tare  alwayt  to  let  it  be  wdl 
aired. 

When  your  nuitreaa  haa  undressed,  examine  all  her  clotbea 
with  great  niceness,  and  if  you  discover  any  spots  on  them,  let 
tbem  be  immediately  taken  out;. after  which  fold  them  up 
carefully,  and  put  them  in  their  proper  places. 

In  order  to  make  the  chambermaid  complete  mistress  of  the 
character  she  assumes,  we  shall  here  add  a  number  of  useful 
receipts,  which,  if  properly  attended  toi,  will  enable  her  to 
discharge  her  office  with  satisfaction  to  her  mistress,  and 
reputation  to  herself. 

To  take  Spots  out  of  Silk. 
Scrape  a  piece  of  chalk  very  fine,  lay  aome  of  it  on  the  spot, 
and  rub  it  gently,  and  you  will  soon  £nd  it  disappear.  Bat 
the  most  effectual  application  is  spirits  of  turpentine  ;  for  thia, 
be  the  spots  ever  so  numerous,  will  infallibly  take  them  ouL 
If  the  first  application  should  not  entirely  effect  it,  the  seccnd 
Wdl.     ' 

TocUanSUktof  aUSorti. 
After  yon  have  thoroughly  taken  out  the  spots,  take  about 
a  peck  of  bran,  dry-it  well  by  the  fire,  then  spread  your  silks 
on  a  convenient  place,  and  rub  them  well  with  the  bran  while 
it  is  warm ;  after  which  shake  it  well  off,  and  rub  them  with  a 
piece  of  clean,  soft,  dry  doth. 

If  the  silks  be  flowered,  take  the  crumb  of  a  stale  three- 
penny loaf,  mix  it  with  about  a  qiurter  of  an  ounce  of  powdor 
blue,  cmmble  them  well  together,  rub  it  gently  over  the  nlk 
with  your  hands,  and  then  with  a  piece  of  clean  doth,  as  for 
plain  silks.

ADYICB  TO  PSMALB  SSRVAKTS.  606 

quantity  of  fuller's  earthy  and  then  scour  your  silks  with  it

To  take  Spots  out  of  Linen, 

There  are  several  methods  of  taking  spots  out  of  linen ;  but 
.  the  most  effectual  are  the  following.  Take  some  juice  of  sorrel, 
heat  it  well  over  the  fire,  and  dip  the  parts  affected  into  it; 
then  rub  it  gently,  and  the  spots  will  vanish.  If  it  be  .  sum- 
mer time,  and  the  sun  very  powerful,  soap  the  places  where 
the  spots  are,  and  hang  it  in  the  sun,  and  when  it  is  dry  the 
spots  will  be  gone.  Or,  rub  some  salt  and  vinegar  well  on 
them,  after  which  squeeze  it  well  out,  and  then  let  it  dry 
gently  by  the  fire.  If  the  linen  be  stained  with  paint,  rub 
some  butter  over  the  spot,  hang  it  in  the  sun  to  dry,  then  wash 
it  well,  and  it  will  all  come  out. 

To  take  Spots  out  of  Crimson  Velvet* 

Take  some  very  strong  aqua  vitas,  and  rub  it  well  on  the 
parts  where  the  spots  are,  and  then  take  the  white  of  a  new 
laid  egg,  spread  it  over  the  aqua  vitae,  and  put  it  in  the  sun  to 
dry.  When  this  is  done,  wash  it  in  clean  water,  and  wring  it 
thoroughly  dry.  You  need  not  be  afraid,  as  it  will  not  do  the 
least  injury  to  the  colour. 

To  take  Spots  out  of  St^ffs,  or  Cloth. 

Take  some  of  the  clearest  and  whitest  fuller's  earth  you  can 
meet  with,  let  it  be  well  dried  by  the  fire,  after  which  pound 
it  in  a  mortar  till  it  be  very  fine;  then  mix  some  spirits  of 
turpentine  with  it,  and  form  it  into  round  balls,  which  you 
may  keep  by  you  to  use  as  occasion  shall  require.  Take  a 
piece  of  one  of  these  balls,  put  it  into  a  cup  or  pan,  mix  a  little 
boiling  water  with  it,  and  lay  it  on  the  parts  that  are  spotted. 
When  it  is  dry,  rub  it  with  a  little  hard  brush,  and  when  the 
spots  are  out,  take  a  clean  bit  of  cloth,  and  rub  it  gently  till 
y  ou  have  taken  the  fuller's  earth  out  also. 

To  take  Spots  out  of  Scarlet.

To  take  Spots  of  Ink  or  Wine  out  of  Cloth,  or  Unen.

lemon  juice,  and  it  will  take  the  spot  quite  out  Or  if  you 
take  a  little  essential  salt  of  lemons,  and  put  a  little  over  the 
jpu^  ^ned,  with  a  tea-spoon,  while  over  the  boiling  water,

careful  not  to  let  this  touch  any  thing  coloured,  as  it  viO 
destroy  the  colour. 

To  take  out  all  Sorts  qf  Spots  or  Stains  from  the  Hands,  ^c.

To  make  Linen  White  that  is  turned  Yellauf.

some  time,  take  them  out,  put  them  into  a  lather  of  hot  water, 
and  wash  them  out.

The  heU  Method  of  Whitening  any  Sort  of  Cloth. 

PiMt,  let  your  doth  be  well  bucked,  then  sprei^  it  on  the 
grass,  and  sprinkle  it  with  alum  water.  Let  it  continue  in  ibh 
situation  for  three  or  four  days;  then  buck  it  again  with  soap 
and  fuller's  earth,  and  use  it  as  before ;  by  which  means  it  will 
not  only  grow  white,  but  swell  in  iU  substance.

To  clean  Ribban$> 

First,  sprinkle  them  moderately  with  a  little  dean  water, 
and  then  lay  them  on  a  carpet  or  clean  doth  at  full  iNreadth,

To  wash  Silk  Stockings.  . 

These  must  not  be  laid  in  soak  before  washing,  as  it  will 
entirely  destroy  their  colour.  They  must  be  washed  in  cold 
water  with  two  lathers,  the  latter  of  whidi  must  be  well  blued. 
They  must  not  be  rinsed,  but  turn  them  often,  then  press 
them,  and  when  they  are  thoroughly  dry,  put  them  up  for  use. 

To  wash  Silk  Handkerchiefs. 

These  must  also  be  first  waslied  in  cdd  water ;  and  the 
second  lather  must  be  only  lukewarm.  After  the  second 
washing,  rinse  them  in  cold  water,  dry  them  gently,  and  then 
fold  them  up. 

To  wash  black  and  whiie  Sarsenets. 

First,  lay  them  smooth  on  a  board  or  carpet,  spreading  a 
little  soap  over  the  dirty  place ;  then  make  a  lather  with  Cas* 
tile  soap,  and  having  an  indifferently  fine  brush,  dip  it  therein, 
and  rub  over  the  silks  the  right  way, .  viz.  longways,  and 
repeat  this  till  you  find  that  side  is  sufficiently  scoured  ;  then 
turn  the  other,  and  use  it  in  the  same  manner ;  after  whidi 
put  it  into  dean  water  scalding  hot.  When  it  has  been  in  this 
some  time,  mix  a  small  quantity  of  gum  arabic  in  some  cold 
water,  and  rinse  thejn  well ;  then  take  them  out  and  fold  them, 
clapping  or  pressing  out  the  water  with  your  hands  on  a  cnpet 
that  is  dry ;  when  you  find  the  water  pretty  well  out.  in  esse  of 
the  white,  yon  must  have  some  brimstone  ready  to  smokc^  or

The  best  Method  of  making  and  using  Starch. 

■  * 

Take  such  a  quantity  of  starch  as  you  think  proportionable 
to  the  things  you  have  to  use  it  for^  just  moisten  it  with  a  little 
water,  and  then  mix  a  small  quantity  of  powder  bUie  with  it^ 
af^r  which  put  it  into  about  half  a  pint  of  water,  and  stir  it 
well  together.  Hare  about  a  quart  of  water  boiling  on  the 
fire,  and  when  your  starch  and  blue  is  sufficiently  stirred  up, 
put  it  into  the  water  as  it  boils.  Let  it  boil  for  about  a  quarter 
of  an  hour,  and  be  sure  to  keep  it  stirring  all  the  while.  The. 
more  it  is  stirred  the  stifier  it  will  be,  and  your  linen  will  look 
the  better. 

Those  things  you  woul4  have  most  stiffened  must  be  dipped 
in  first ;  you  must  not  mb  the  starch  too  strong,  you  may 
weaken  it  by  the  addition  of  a  little  water ;  and  before  yOu 
nse  it  be  sure  to  let  it  be  well  strained. 

You  should  always  boil  your  starch  in  a  copper  vessel,  be* 
cause  as  it  requires  a  deal  of  boiling,  tin  is  very  apt  to  make  it 
burn  too. 

There  are  various  things  which  different  people  mix  with 
their  starch,  such  as  alum,  gum  arabic,  and  tallow;  but  ifyou 
do  put  any  thing  in»  let  it  be  a  little  isinglass,  for  that  is  by  far 
the  best.  About  an  ounce  to  a  quarter  of  a  pound  of  starch 
will  be  sufficient. 

To  wash  Thread  and  Cotton  Stockings, 

Both  these  must  have  two  lathers  and  a  boil,  and  the  water 
must  be  well  blued.  When  this  is  done,  wash  them  out  of  the 
boil,  after,  which  fold  them  up  very  smooth  without  rinsing, 
and  press  them  under  a  weight  for  about  half  an  hour :  then 
hang  them  up,  and  wlien  they  are  thoroughly  dry,  roll  them 
up  without  ironing,  and  they  will  look  lUi  well  as  when  new. 

Worsted  stockings  must  be  washed  in  two  lathers  till  they 
are  quite  clean,  but  do  not  put  any  soap  on  them :  when  you 
have  done  this,  rinse  them  well,  hang  them  up,  and  as  soon  as 
ihey  are  dry,  fold  them  up  for  use.

To  dean  Silk  Furniture.

To  clean  Damask  Curtains^  and  other  Sorts  of   Worsted 

Furniture, 

Take  some  dry  fuller's  earth  of  the  whitest  sort  you  caa 
procure  ;*  when  it  is  well  dried  before  the  fire,  pour  boiling 
water  upon  it,  until  it  is  quite  soft ;  then  put  to  it  two  penny- 
worth of  the  spirits  of  turpentine,  and  when  they  are  all  pro- 
perly mixed,  lay  your  curtains  or  other  cloths  on  a  large  square 
table,  and  rub  it  upon  them  as  hard  as  yon  can,  remembering 
always  to  rub  them  first  on  the  inside,  and  then  on  the  oat; 
rub  every  place  over,  and  then  hang  them  up  on  a  line,  either 
in  the  air  or  before  the  fire :  when  they  are  quite  dry,  bruA 
them  with  a  hard  brush,  and  then  with  two  softer  <»iea,  and 
the  curtains  will  look  extremely  wdl.

To  rinse  MtuKns-befmrepouitarch  them.

any  wet  is  left  in  them.

To  clap  MtuUfu  before  they  are  ironed.

INSTRUCTIONS  TO  HOUSEMAIDS. 

The  business  of  the  housemaid  is  to  look  after  and  keep 
the  ftimiture  clean,  in  the  execution  of  which  she  is  to  take 
her  instructions  from  the  housekeeper;  and  if  she  would  wish 
to  acquire  reputation  she  must  be  industrious  and  deanly. 

Eveijr  morning  her  first  business  must  be,  in  summer,  to 
rub  the  stoves  and  fire  irons  with  scouring  paper,  and  to  dean 
the  hearths.  In  winter,  she  must  first  rake  out  the  ashes^  and 
sweep  the  grate  very  dean ;  she  must  then  clean  the  irons, 
which,  if  the  common  sort,  may  be  done  by  rubbing  them. 


614  DOMESTIC- COOKIRT* 

first  with  a  rag  dipped  in  vinegar  and  tbe  ■ahes*  then  with  an 
oily  rag,  and  after  that  with  soouriog  paper«  rottOHrtcoe,  «r 
white  brick.  If  there  be  fine  steel  stovei  and  teders^  they 
should  be  first  rubbed  with  oil,  then  with  emery » till  clear  and 
bright^  and  next  with  scouring  paper*  This  is  also  an  excdlent 
paper  to  rub  irons  with  that  are  not  in  CQQstaot  use,  evcsy  two 
or  three  days,  as  it  will  take  off  any  spots  they  may  have  got 
in  that  time.  When  she  has  thus  prepared  the  stoye,  she  may 
light  the  fire,  and  wash  the  hearth  and  chimney-piece.  If  the 
latter  be  marble,  washing  it  once  a  week  is  sufficient,  whidi 
should  be  done  with  a  piece  of  flannel  dipped  in  a  lather  of 
hot  water  and  soap.  The  hearth  ought  to  be  cleaned  every 
morning.  Hearths  and  chimney-sides  of  steel  must  be  cleaned 
in  the  same  mannec4B  ^^^  ^^^1  stoves. 

After  the  fire-pfasM^  the  housemaid's  nixt  business  is  to  dean 
the  locks  of  the  doors.  In  doing  this  she  must  have  a  piece 
of  pasteboard  for  each,  with  a  hole  cut  in  it  just  big  enon^ 
for  slipping  over  the  lock,  to  preserve  the  doors,  to  .which  the 
same  side  of  the  pasteboard  should  always  be  applied,  finr 
the  dirty  side  .would  spoil  them.  The  locks  may  be  cleaned 
by  rubbing  them  with  an  oily  rag,  and  next  with  rotten-stoBe 
or  white  brick ;  but  she  must  be  very  careful  not  to  let  any 
of  the  two  last  get  into  the  key-hole.  Lacko^  locks  want 
no  other  cleaning  but  rubbing  with  a  piece  of  dean  leather  or 
woollen  doth ;  for  oil,  or  any  thing  damp,  hurts  their  odour. 

The  housemaid's  next  attention  should  be  to  thecarpets^ 
which  she  may  sweep  with  a  common  broom,  or  brush  with  a 
whisk  broom,  and  then  fold  them  back ;  after  which  she  ought 
to  sweep  the  room,  having  first  strewed  it  with  sand  pr^ty 
damp.  But,  before  she  sweeps  the  rooms,  she  should  brush 
and  clean  the  window  curtains,  and  with  a  broom  sweep  the 
windows,  and  behind  the  shutters.  She  must  not  apply  a 
brush  or  broom  to  any  pictures  or  frames^  but  only  blow  the 
dust  off  with  a  pair  of  bellows ;  though  she  may  now  and  then 
dust  them  with  a  very  soft  duster,  or  piece  of  flannel;  and 
she  should  also  blow  off  the  dust  ftom  the  wainscot>  china, 
and  stucco  work.  .  . 

When  she  has  swept  the  room,  and  taken  up  the  dust,  her 
next  business  is,  to  rub  the  wainscot  from  the  top  .to  the  bottom 
with  a  duster,  and  do  the  same  to  the  windows.    She  must

thai  sweep  the  stain,  after  which  die  must  dust  the  wainscot 
and  bidusters  directly^  and  also  the  tops  4)f  the  doors. 

As  soon  as  the  fiunily  is  up,  the  housemaid  should  set  open 
the  windows  of  the  bed-chaml)ers»  and  uncover  the  beds  to 
aweelen  and  air  them,  which  will  be  a;  great  help  against  bugs 
and  fleas.  In  making  the.  beds,  she  should  begin  with  that 
first  aired,  taking  off  the  several  things  singly,  and  laying 
them  on  two  chairs^  .  without  letting  -them  touch  the  floor. 
She  should  shake  the  beds  well  every  day,  and  if  there  be  a 
mattress,  let  her  turn  it  at  least  once  a  week.  The  cleaning 
of  the  head  of  the  bed,  the  valances,  and  curtains,  with  a 
brush  or  whisk,  is  not  to  be  omitted ;  neither  should  she  forget 
to  sweep  clean  behind  and  under  the  bedsteads.

To  preterve  Iran  from  Rn$t 

Dissolve  a  quarter  of  an  ounce  of  camphire  and  half  a  pound 
of  hog's  lard  together  over  a  very  slow  fire,  and  taking  off  the 
scum,  mix  as  much  black-lead  as  will  bring  them  to  an  iron 
colour.  Spread  this  composition  over  the  steel  and  iron  stoves, 
as  also  the  fire-irons.  Let  it  lie  on  them  for  twenty-four  hours, 
after  which  rub  them  with  a  dry  linen  doth,  and  they  will 
keep  without  rust  for  six  months. 

To  take  Rust  out  of  Steel. 
Cover  with  sweet  oil  well  rubbed  on  it;  in  forty-eight  hours 
use  unslacked  lime,  powdered  irery  thin.    Rub  it  till  the  rust 
disappears.

on  a  bit  of  broad  doth ;  when  the  dirt  is  removed,  wipe  them 
dean,  and  polish  with  glass,  not  sand  paper.

To  clean  Marble  Chimney-ptecee,  Spc.

To  blacken  theTronts  of  Stone  Chhnney-pieces.

7%e  best  Way  to  clean  a  Room. 

Rub  both  the  brush  and  mop  with  the  grain,  that  is,  with 
the  length  of  the  board,  and  not  across  the  breadth,  neither  let 
the  boards  be  wet  too.  .much,  because  it  soaks  in  and  hurtf 
tbero.  Rub  them  hard  with  fine  dry  sand,  and  take  care  noj^ 
to  wet  too  much  of  the  room  at  one  time;  and  the  sooner  yon 


ADVICE  TO  FBMALB  SBRVANTS.  617 

have  done  it  the  better.  When  too  much  water  is  thrown  cm 
the  boards,  it  takes  up  more  time  to  clean  it  than  if  a  small 
part  was  wetted  at  once,  and  the  boards- will  always  look  more 
black  and  spongy.  When  you  intend  to  dry«rub  it,  let  it  first 
be  quite  dry,  and  then  take  a  doth  and  rub  it  clean,  throwing 
over  it  some  fine  sand,  and  sweep  it  as  clean  as  possible.  Let 
the  skirting  boards  be  rubbed  with  a  piece  of  oily  flannel,  and 
they  will  look  as  if  newly  painted ;  but  no  part  of  the  floor, 
for  that  will  spoil  it.  Neither  fuller's  earth  nor  common  saitd 
ought  to  be  used,  as  they  are  sure  to  make  some  impressiota. 

To  clean  Paint. 

Never  use  a  cloth ;  take  off  the  dust  with  a  little  long*haired 
brush,  after  blowing  off  the  loose  parts  with  the  bellows* 
With  care,  paint  will  look  well  for  a  long  time.  When  soiled^ 
dip  a  sponge  or  bit  of  flannel  into  soda  and  water,  and  wash  it 
off  quickly,  and  dry  it  immediately,  or  tlie  strength  of  the  soda 
will  eat  off  the  colour. 

When  wainscot  is  scoured,  it  should  be  done  from  the  top 
downwards ;  the  suds  should  be  prevented  rom  ruuniog  as 
much  as  possible,  or  it  will  make  marks  that  cannot  be  got  oat. 
One  person  should  dry  with  soft  linen  cloths  as  fast  as  the  other 
has  scoured  off  the  dirt,  and  washed  the  soda  off. 

To  clean  Stairt, 

Stairs  are  to  be  cleaned  in  the  same  manner  as  rooms,  only 
it  is  necessary  to  observe,  that  if  you  keep  your  face  always 
to  the  ascent,  they  will  have  a  much  better  appearance,  and 
you  will  be  more  able  to  do  them  soon  and  well.  Let  the 
haircloth  be  swept  once  every  day,  and  once  a  week  taken  up, 
and  the  dust  shaken  out  of  it ;  then  scour  them  down,  and 
when  diy,  lay  the  cloth  on  again.  When  the  stairs  are  of 
atone,  let  them  be  scoured  with  sand  and  water,  but  boards 
must  be  rubbed  with  a  piece  of  oily  flannel,  and  they  will  look 

if  newly  painted. 


To  clean  Windows.     - 

To  do  this  properly,  there  must  be  two  persons,  one  without 
and  another  within ;  rub  them  over  with  a  thick  damp  cloth, 
and  then  with  a  dry  one;  and  if  any  spots  remain,  do  them

To  clean  Stone  Stairs,  Halls,  Sfc.

To  clean  Oil^cloths  that  are  laid  on  Floon* 
The  best  method  of  keeping  these  in  proper  order  Vk,  to 
dry-rub  them  every  day>  because  it  not  only  keeps  them  dein, 
but  also  preserves  them  belter  than  any  thing  that  can  be 
mentioned,  for  when  mops  are  used  they  soon  wear  out.  Once 
every  week  let  them  be  turned  upside  down;  and  onoe  eveiy 
month  let  them  be  rubbed  over  with  railk^  and  hung  out  to 
dry;  then  let  them  be  rubbed  over  widi  a  clodi^  add  they  wiB 
look  as  well  as  at  the  first. 

To  clean  Floor-cloths. 
Sweep  and  clean  the  floor-cloths  with  a  broom  and  damp 
flannel,  in  the  usual  nuinner ;  then  wet  them  alL,over  with 
milk,  and  rub  them  till  bright  with  a  dry  doth.  They  will 
thus  look  as  well  as  if  they  were  rubbed  with  a  waxed  flannel, 
without  being  so  slippery,  or  so  soon  clogged  with  dust  or  dirt.

To  clean  Carpets. 

Take  up  the  carpet,  and  let  it  be  well  beaten,  then  laid 
down,  and  brushed  on  both  sides  with  a  hand-brush ;  turn  it 
the  right  side  upwards,  and  scour  it  with  gall  and  soap  and 
water  very  clean,  and  dry  it  with  linen  cloths.  Then  lay  it  on 
the  gtass,  or  hang  it  up  to  dry. 

To  dust  Carphts  and  Floors. 

Sprinkle  tea-leaves,  then  sweep  carefully.  Carpets  should 
not  be  Bwept  frequently  with  a  whisk  brush,  as  it  wears  them 
very  fast;  about  once  a  week  is  sufficient;  at  other  times  use 
tea-leaves  and  a  hair  brush. 

To  clean  Chairs. 

Drop  some  linseed  oil  upon  a  woollen  rag,  and  rub  the  chairs 
Willi  it,  imd  then  rub  them  hard  with  a  dry  cloth  until  they 
iqpfpear  bright ;  then  rub  some  yellow  wax  on  a  hard  brush, 
and  brush  them  all  over;  then  take  a- rough  woollen*  cloth,  and 
again  nib  tihem';  and  they  will  look  as  well  as  when  new.

ToelecnTiMes.

An  useful  Receipt  to  take  Spots  out  of  Boards  and  large  TMes- 

Make  some  lye  of  wood  ashes,  and  mix  it  with  a  few  galls, 
•then  put  it  on  the  spots  the  evening  before  you  intend  to  dean 
them.  In  the  morning  rub  the  boards  hard  with  a  brush,  and 
if  it  is  a  floor  you  must  do  it  on  your  knees.  Let  it  be  with 
the  grain,  and  take  some  fine  sand  at  the  second  soouiiiq^; 
when  they  are  dry,  take  a  coarse  woollen  cloth,  and  rub  diem 
clean,  until  you  see  no  spots  remaining.  When  you  have 
brought  them  to  a  right  colour,  and  can  distinguish  the  grain, 
then  wash  them  with  cold  water  and  sand.  Hot  water  mual 
not  be  used,  as  it  opens  the  grain  of  the  boards,  and  hard 
water  always  spoils  the  colour. 

To  poUsk  Mahogany  Tables,  ^c. 

Take  a  quarter  of  an  ounce  of  the  finest  white  soap,  grate 
it  small,  and  put  it  into  a  new  glazed  earthen  vessel,  with  a  pint 
of  water ;  hold  it  over  the  fire  till  the  soap  is  dissolved ;  then 
add  the  same  quantity  of  bleached  wax  cut  into  small  pieces, 
and  three  ounces  of  common  lyax ;  as  soon  as  the  whole  ia  in- 
corporated, it  is  fit  for  use. 

Wh^  you  use  it;,  clean  the  table  well,  dip  abit  of  flannd  in 
the  varnish  while  warm,  and  nib  it  on  the  table;  let  it  stand  a 
qonuter  of  an  hour,  then  apply  die  hard  brush  in  all  directioni, 
and  finish  with  a  bit  of  dean  dry  flannel.  This  will  prodim  a 
gloss  like  a  mirror.  > 

To  take  Ink  Stains  out  of  Mahogsmj/. 
Put  a  few  drops  of  spirit  of  sea^t,  or  oil  of  vitriol,  in  a 
tea-spoonful  of  water:  touch  the  stain  or  qpot  with  a  feather; 
and,  on  the  ink's  disappearing  rub  it  over  immediatdj  with  a 
rag  wetted  in  cold  water,  or  there  will  be  a  white  marie  which 
win  not  be  easily  effaced.

To  give  a  fine  Colour  to  Mahogany* 

'  Ink  and  other  stains  being  removed^  wash  the  fumitore  with 
vinegar^  and  then  nib  it  all  over  with  a  red  mixture  made  in 
the  following  manner  :^Put  into  it  a  pint  of  cold-drawn  linseed 
oil^  four  penny  weMh  of  alkanet  root,  and  two  of  rose-pink ; 
stir  them  well  together  in  an  earthen  vessel,  and  let  them  re- 
main all  nighty,  when  the  mixture^  being  again  well  stirred, 
will  be  immediately  fit  for  use.  After  it  has  been  left  an  hour 
on  the  furniture,  it  may  be  rubbed  off  with  linen  cloths  till 
briB^t;  it  will  soon  have  a  beantifiil  colour  as  well  as  a  glossy 
appearance. 

To  clean  old  Pictures. 

Take  two  ounces  of  borax,  and  a  quarter  of  an  ounce  of 
Soman  vitriol^  beat  them  together  till  they  are  very  small,  then 
let  thein  be  sifted  through  a  fine  lawn  sieve:  when  you  have 
rubbed  the  dust  off  the  picture,  then  lay  it  flat  on  the  ground^ 
and  throw  some  of  the  powder  over  the  canvas ;  dip  a  brush  in 
water,  and  mb  the  picture  over  carefully  until  it  is  quite  dean* 
Take  no  more  water  than  will  wet  the  powder,  and  when  you 
find  the  picture  begins  to  appear  clean,  wash  off  the  dust  with 
ft  wet  brush,  and  set  it  up  to  dry  in  a  place  not  too  much 
exposed  to  the  sun.  Then  take  a  litde  linseed  oil,  and  when 
the  picture  begins  to  be  dry,  rub  it  over  with  a  feather  dipped 
in  the  oil.  Don't  let  them  dry  too  soon,  for  the  longer  they 
stand  they  will  have  the  more  transparent  colour. 

To  clean  and  freeerve  Qildiug. 

It  is  quite  impossible  to  prevent  the  flies  from  staining  the 
gilding  without  oovering  it;  before  which,  blow  off  the  light 
dust,  and  let  a  feather  or  clean  brush  pass  over  it:  then  with 
strips  of  paper  cover  the  frames  of  your  glasses,  and  do  not 
reni^ve  it  till  the  flies  are  gone. 

linen  takes  off  the  gilding,  and  deadens  its  brightness :  it 
should  therefore  never  be  used  to  it. 

Some  means  should  be  used  to  destroy  the  fltes,  as  they 
injure  finrnitore  of  every  kind,  as  well  as  the  paper.  Bottles 
httig  i^ut  with  sugar  and  vinegar,  or  beer,  will  attract  them. 
Or,  fly-waSsr,  pm  into  the  bottom  of  a  saucer,  should  be  used.

INSTRUCTIONS  TO  LAUNDRY-MAIDS. 

As  the  lavndryimaid  is  the  person  -to  whom  ikte  care  of  the 
linen  is  oommitted,  it  ia  moat  common  for  her  to  be  brought 
up  to  it ;  but  yet  any  young  wt>nan  of  tdlerable  dblKties  may 


824  DOMESTIC  COOKERY. 

ioon  learn  it,  bb  all  women  are  more  or  less  aoqaainted  wiik 
washing.  Where  linen  is  either  badly  washed,  or  not  properly 
got  up,  it  soon  wears;  and  one  bad  washing  does  more  hurt 
than  ten  times  using  it.  See  that  every  part  of  the  linen  be 
mended  properly  before  you  begin  to  wash,  and  when  washed, 
let  it  be  done  upas  soon  as  possible,  otherwise  it  will  be  apt  to 
assume  a  bad  colour,  so  that  your  mistress  will  certainly  com- 
plain. Be  extremely  regular  in  your  stated  days  for  washings 
and  never  take  a  woman  to  assist  you  without  leave  from  year 
mistress.  Let  all  your  tubs  and  other  vesseb  be  kept  clean, 
and  never  waste  soap  or  any  other  materials,  but  use  them  with 
the  same  frugality  as  if  they  had  been  purchased  by  yourself. 
It  is  the  practice  in  many  parts  of  England,  for  the  laundry- 
maids  to  rise  very  early,  and  moat  certainly  on  the  washing  day 
it  is  best,  because,  they  will  have  the  work  over  before  the 
evening ;  but  on  the  other  days  they  may  enjoy  equal  indal* 
gence  with  their  fellow-servants. 

Having  said  thus  much  relative  to  the  laundry-nuud,  we 
shall  now  lay  down  such  further  directions  as  may  easily  asost 
her  in  ^e  executioti  of  her  budness.  , 

To  prepare  l,inen  far  Washing, 

First  look  the  linen  carefully  ov^,  and  then  mend  erery 
place  where  you  find  it  torn,  otherwise  if  it  is  washed  the  r^its 
will  be  much  worse  than  before.  When  that  is  done,  let  ft 
be  carefully  folded  up,  and  put  into  a  bag,  to  prevent  its 
gathering,  more  dirt,  for  the  cleaner  it  is  kept,  the  mere  easily 
it  will  wash,  and  also  be  the  better  for  the  linen ;  for  as  much 
has  been  lost  *by  the  carelessness  of  servants,  or  bad  laundiy- 
maids,  as  by  wearing. 

C&nceming  the  Water. 

Do  not  wash  with  any  sort  of  water,  unless  it  has  stood  two 
or  three  days,  for  when  ifewly  taken  in,  it  is  always  thick  and 
muddy;  if  it  is  from  a  stream  that  has  a  muddy  bottom,  ft 
will  be  better  to  let  it  stand  four  days. 

Concerning  Soap. 

New  made  soap  always  spoils  the  linen,  theiefiire  make 
choice  of  the  oldest  you  can  get,  as  it  will  be  of  much '  moie 
servfoe,  and  make  Ae  dothei  look  better.

Sdda^  by  softening  the  water,  saves  a  great  deal  of  soap, 
ft  should  be  melted  in  a  large  jug  of  water,  some  of  which 
pottr  into  the  tubs  and  boiler ;  and  when  the  lather  becomes 
weak,  add  more.  The  new  improvement  in.  soft  soap  is,  if 
properly  used,  a  saving  of  near  half  in  quantity ;  and  though 
something  dearer  than  die  -hard,  reduces  iSie  price  of  washing 
consuta^bly. 

Many  good  laundresses  advise  seafMng  linen  in  warm  v/wMt 
the  night  previous  to  washing,  as  facilitating  the  operatioil 
with  less  fiiction. 

Soap  sho«ild  be  aat  widi  a  wire  or  twine.  In  pieces  that  will 
make  a  long  sqoaM,  when  lirst  brought  in,  and  kept  oilt  of  liie 
mir  two  or  duree  weeks ;  for  if  it  dry  quick,  ii  wUl  crack,  and 
when  wet,  bveak.  Put  it  on  a  shetf,  leaving  a  spice  between, 
and  let  it  grow  hard  gradually.  Thns  it  will  save  a  Aill  third 
in  the  consumption. 

Direction  eonceming  W4igking. 
Wfato  you  have  cleaned  your  copper,  fill  it  to  heat,  and 
wbaa  you  have  aorted  your  idflihef  properly,  let  ibiem  be 
rubbed  over  with  soap,  taking  care  to  put  most  on  the  dirty 
places,  and  then  wash  the  finest  first.  Let  not  the  water  be 
too  hot.  When  you  have  washed  the  fine  linen,  take  it  out 
and  lay  it  on  a  fdean  place ;  then  wash  the  coarse,  which  will 
take  a  good  deed  mare  washing  than  i(he  other;  then  soap  the 
Im^n  over  again,  and  kt  it  be  washed  a  second  time  in  water 
more  hot  than  the  form^. 

To  bail  Linen. 

As  soon  as  you  have  put  the  water  on,  mix  with  it  some 
stone  blue,  and  when  you  have  soaped  the  linen,  let  it  bp  put 
in  to  boil ;  when  it  has  boiled  ten  or  fifteen  mimrtes,  according 
to  the  n«ture  of  the  linen,  for  that  which  is  coarse  will  tabe 
mueh  B)ore  tham  the  fine,  then  pat  the  water  with  the  linen 
into  the  tub,  and  let  it  stand  till  it  is  cold  enough  for  you  to 
hold  your  hand  in  it ;  then  wash  the  linen  quite  clean,  taking 
care  n^t  Uf  kave  any  pieeea  of  spap  upon  it,  for  if  you  do  it 
will  look  greasy.  As  soon  as  you  have  washed  the  different 
pieces,  let  them  be  thrown  into  clear  pump- water,  mixed  with 
stone  blue,  then  rinse  it  perfectly  clean,  and  when  you  have

&26  DOMESTIC  COOKERY. 

wrung  it^  hang  up  the  different  pieces  of  linen  at  a  moderate 
distaqce  from  each  other;  and  when  they  are  dry,  fold  them 
carefully  up  until  they  are  ironed^  which  must  be  as  soon  sui 
you  conveniently  can. 

Directions  for  Ironing. 

If  you  use  flat-irons^  be  sure  to  rub  them  smooth  against  a 
mat,  until  they  are  very  bri^t,  and  then  rub  them  with  a 
smooth  flannel,  which  must  be  done  every  time  they  come  to 
the  fire.  It  will  be  better  for  the  linen,  that  you  use  the  iron 
as  hot  as  you  can,  only  take  care  to  try  it  on  a  rag,  lest  it 
damage  the  lindn.  Sprinkle  a  few  drops  of  water  upon  the 
linen  before  you  begin  to  iron,  always  taking  care  to  put  more 
water  to  the  fine  than  the  coarse.  The  water  makes  it  more 
pliable  in  ironing,  and  more  stiff  afterwards. 

To  take  Stains  made  by  Fruit  out  of  Unen. 

Take  some  freah  butter,  and  rub  it  over  all  the  stains,  then 
put  it  into  a  tub,  and  pour  upon  it  scalding  milk  ;  when  it  is 
cold,  let  the  stains  be  washed  with  the  milk  until  they  are 
perfectly  out. 

To  take  out  Spots  made  hy  Ink. 

Put  the  stained  places  in  vin^ar  mixed  with  suet,  where  it 
must  be  all  night;  wash  it  in  the  mor&ing  in  the  vin^ar,  and 
you  will  see  the  spots  become  more  dull  and  faint ;  then,  put 
more  vinegar  to  it,  and  when  it  has  laid  another  night,  let  it 
be  washed  in  the  same  manner,  and  the  spots  will  infidlibly 
vanish* 

To  take  out  the  Mildew. 

Mix  soft  soap  with  starch  powdered^  half  as  much  salt,  and 
the  juiceof  a  lemon;  lay  it  on  the  part  on  both  sides  with  a 
painter's  brush.  Let  it  lie  on  the  grass  day  and  night  till  the 
stain  comes  out 

To  make  Flannels  keep  their  Colour  and  not  shrink. 

Put  them  into  a  pail,  and  pour  boiling  water  on,  ktting 
them  lie  till  cold,  the  first  time  of  washing.

INSTRUCTIONS  TO  COOK-MAIDS. 

X  HE  knowledge  of  cookery  is  essentially  necessary  to  every 
'woman,  unless  her  station  in  life '  is  of  very  elevated  rank. 
She  who  undertakes  to  be  cook  in  a  family  ought  to  be  well 
acquainted  with  the  qualities  of  provisions  in  general^  and  also 
of  the  most  proper  methods  used  in  dressing  them,  which  is 
first  acquired  by  attending  to  proper  directions  laid  down  for 
that  purpose,  and  then  the  reducing  of  those  to  practice.  The 
importance  of  a  good  kitcken-doctress,  or  cook,  is  clearly  illus- 
trated by  the  following  interesting  observations,  fr6m  the  pen 
of  a  celebrated  physician. 

'  The  stomach/  says  Dr.  Hunter,  '  is  the  chief  org^n  of  the 
human  system,  upon  the  state  of  which  all  the  powers  and 
feelings  of  the  individual  depend.  The  stomach  is  the  kitchen 
that  prepareb  our  discordant  food,  and  which,  after  due  mace- 
ration,  it  delivers  over,  by  a  certain  undulatory  motion,  to  the 
ihtestines,  when  it  receives  a  further  concoction.  Being  now 
reduced  into  a  white  balmy  fluid,  it  is  sucked  up  by  a  set  of 
small  vessels,  called  lacteals,  and  carried  to  the  thoracic  duct. 
This  duct  runs  up  the  back  bone,  and  is  in  length  about 
sixteen  inches,  but  in  diameter  it  hardly  exceeds  a  crow-quill. 
Through  this  small  tube  the  greater  part  of  what  is  taken  in 
at  Ae  mouth  passes,  and  is  discharged  into  the  left  subclavian 
vein,  when  mixing  with  the  general  mass  of  blood  it  becomes 
blood  itself.' 

A  cook-maid  ought  to  keep  herself  neat  and  clean,  be  care- 
fbl  in  attending  to  her  business, '  and  never  intrust  any  person 
with  doing  part  of  her  work  unless  in  her  presence,  that  she 
may  see  that  things  are  properly  conducted ;  for  when  any  sort 
of  victuals  is  spoiled,  the  cook  is  sure  to  be  blamed.  She  knows 
how  victuals  are  dressed  when  she  sees  them  done,  but  she 
cannot  answer  for  what  is  dressed  out  of  her  sight.  She 
ought  also  to  take  great  care  of  the  meat,  and  never  encourage 
people  to  come  after  her ;  nor  give  any  away  unless  she  .first 
obtain  the  consent  of  her  lady  or  housekeeper.  It  is  a  sort  of 
theft  to  give  that  away  which  is  not  her  own ;  but  if  she  attend 


0*28  SK>MBSTIC  COOKBRY. 

to  the  above  rules  she  will  acquire  the  good-will  of  all  who 
know  her,  and  on  every  future  occasion  have  an  unblemished 
character  from  her  lady. 


DIRECTIONS  TO  KITCHEN-MAIDS. 

JSy  whatever  means  either  man  or  woman  procures  a  Uveli- 
hood,  if  it  is  but  in  an  honest  way,  they  ought  not  to  be  treat- 
ed with  contempt :  for  we  are  all  as  so  many  links  of  the  same 
chain ;  every  one  contributes  to  the  support  of  his  neighbour  ; 
and  the  woman  who  does  the  most  servile  work  in  a  family,  is 
entitled  to  respect  in  proportion  as  her  service  is  labarionsL 
Let  the  young  woman,  therefore,  who  is  obliged  to  submit  to 
that  drudgery^  be  content  with  the  station  in  which  ProvideDce 
has  placed  her,  without  repining,  always  remembering  that 
humility  is  the  road  to  preferment,  and  the  more  submissive 
she  is  in  a  low  station,  the  better  she  will  be  qualified  for  aa 
devated  one. 

As  the  scullery-maid's  busiqess  is  to  keep  the  different  rooms, 
such  as  kitchen,  paptry,  wash-house,  &c.  dean,  so  it  ia  her 
duty  to  take  great  care  that  nothing  be  lost  from  them^  nor 
any  stranger  admitted ;  for  if  things  be  lost  the  blame  wiU 
naturally  fall  on  her,  although  she  may  be  innocent.  Let  her 
take  great  care  that  all  the  dishes  and  other  things  committed  to 
her  care  be  kept  in  proper  order,  so  that  when  the  cook  or 
any  of  the  servants  want  them,  they  may  always  be  deap,  and 
ready  for  immediate  use. 

She  must  also  be  very  careful  of  her  coppers  and  brass  veaels. 
These,  immediately  after  use,  should  be  filled  with  water^ 
(which  prevents  the  tinning  from  coming  off)  and  afterwards 
wiped  and  dried ;  for  if  they  be  pot,  they  gather^  as  well  when 
empty  as  when  fat  is  left  in  them,  a  green  substance,  which  is 
rank  poison,  or  at  least  causes  terrible  and  lasting  di9pppden, 
especially  to  those  who  eat  first  what  is  dressed  in  then. 


ADVICB  TO  V9MAIM  8BRVANTS. 


Qcqpper  and  bri^  flpoooe^  especially  thoae  called  white  mctat 
spoons,  ahould  also  be  takeif  particiilar  care  of;  for  they  gathes 
a  greenish  poison,  and  n<»lhing  abpiild  at  any  rate  be  warmed 
ia  tiwm  over  a  fire«  Srolhs  and  soups  should  not  be  left  longer 
standing  in  Ae  porridge-pot,  than  while  dinner  is  taking  ap» 
Fixed  cej^^s  should  hard  the  fire  drawn  from  under  tbem  as 
soon  as  they  are  used,  and  scoured  with  a  brush  and  sand 
whilst  boll.  The  «uitsides  of  tinned  coppetf  utensib  should  be 
also  sqottBSd  with  a  bmsh  and  sand ;  but  not  the  insides,  for 
the  sand  will  take  off  the  tin,  from  whidi  any  specks  may  be 
removed  by  scraping  with  the  nails.  The  dressers  should  be 
scrubbed  with  water  and  soap,  or  wood  ashes,  either  of  them 
being  preferable  to  sand  or  fuller's  earth,  on  account  of  their 
grittiness ;  and  it  should.be  a  constant  maxim  not  to  throw  the 
dirty  waters  down  the  sewen,  if  it  can  be  conveniently  carried 
into  the  street;  for  it  ia  very  apt  to  stop  the  drains,  and  cause 
a  disagreeable  smell. 

Wh^i  the  kitchen-maid  has  finidied  her  work  for  the  day^* 
let  her  be  sure  to  make  herself  clean,  but  never  attempt  ta 
dress  above  her  station,  nor  refuse  her  assistance  to  any  of  Ae 
othtt  servants. 


DIRECTIONS  TO  NURSERY-MAIDS. 

JuLTHOUGH  wehavcj  for  the  sake  of  connexion  and  con- 
veniency,  placed  the  nursery-maid  after  those  engaged  in  house- 
hold work,  yet  the  dignity  of  her  situation  ought  to  rank  her 
very  high  in  the  esteem  of  her  employers.  Indeed,  the  nursing 
and  rearing  of  children  is  a  business  of  tbe  highest  importance, 
as  their  future  health  and  happiness  essentially  depend  on  the 
judgment  and  attention  of  their  nurses.  You  must  therefore 
consider  well  the  nature  of  the  charge  committed  to  your 
care,  and  always  act  with  such  caution,  fidelity,  and  tendemeaa^ 
as  may  secure  the  approbation  of  your  own  conscience,  and  the 


1130  DOMESTIC  COOKBRT. 

lasting  esteem  of  those,  whose  minds  first  received  impressions 
of  virtue  and  gratitude  from  your  judicious  instructions. 

Nurses  from  ignorance  are  frequently  betrayed  into  a  variety 
of  fiital  mistakes,  which  we  shall  endeavour  to  point  out  This 
important  isubject  ought  to  be  carefully  studied  by  all  oonsd- 
entious  nurses,  as  we  are  sure  it  will  be  perused  with  pleasure 
by  eveiy  tender  and  radohal  mother.     - 

Nurses  should  be  endowed  with  such  a  portion  of  good 
sense,  as  may  render  them  capable  of  profiting  by  observation 
and  experience.  Some  dangerous,  though  well-meaning  crea- 
tures, follow  implicitly  the  example  and  transmitted  custmns  of 
their  great-grandmothers,  and  firmly  believe  that  certain  herbs, 
roots,  and  drugs,  possess  strange  unaccountable  powers,  and 
that  some  superstitious  practices  and  ceremonies  will  infallibly 
produce  the  most  miraculous  effects.  Those  nurses  who 
unfortunately  give  credit  to  these  traditional  prejudices,  keep 
dosing  poor  iufants  with  drugs  upon  every  trifling  occsision, 
and  place  more  reliance  upon  some  pernicious  nostrum,  than 
on  their  best  endeavours.  These  medicines  do  mischief  twenty 
times  for  once  they  do  good  ;  and  ought  never  to  be  adminis- 
tered secretly,  and  without  the  order  of  the  parents  or  of  a 
r^fular  physician. 

It  is  absolutely  necessary  to  have  the  nursery  lofty  and 
spacious,  dry  rather  than  warm,  and  exposed  to  the  sun's 
morning  rays.  The  upper  sash  in  fine  weather  ought  always 
to  be  let  down,  to  admit  fresh  air.  In  almost  all  cases  a  child, 
when  above  a  month  old,  should  every  day  be  carried  from  the 
nursery  into  greeu  fields  and  upon  sunny  eminences.  There 
he  will  drink,  as  it  were,  the  vital  stream  pure  from  its  source; 
he  will  draw  in  at  every  breath  fresh  supplies  of  strength  and 
alacrity ;  while  the  bracing  action  of  the  air  on  the  surfiice  of 
his  body,  will  give  the  degree  of  firmness  unattainable  by  any 
other  means. 

In  the  course  of  a  few  months,  the  state  of  the  weather  need 
not  be  mudi  regarded ;  and  its  unfavourable  changes,  unless  the 
heat  or  cold  be  intense,  must  not  operate  as  a  check  ou  those 
daily  excursions  from  the  nursery.  Our  climate  is  very  fickle; 
and  we  shall  suffer  much  from  its  rapid  variations,  if  we  are 
not  fireely  exposed  to  them  in  early  life ;  do  not  therefore  sacri- 


ADVICE  TO  FEMALE  SERVANTS.  ^1 

ficethe  fiitture  safety  and  comfort  of  the  grown  man^  to  mis- 
taken  tenderness  for  the  infant.  If  the  child  be  accustomed 
firbm  the  cradle  to  go  out  in  all  weathers^  he  will  have  nothing 
to  fear  from  the  bleak  north  or  the  sultry  souths  but  will  bear 
every  change  of  season^  of  climate^  and  of  atmosphere,  not  only 
without  danger,  but  without  pain  or' inconvenience. 

The  nurse  or  child's  maid  must,  however,  be  careful  to  keep 
the  children  in  motion  whDe  abroad,  and  not  to  allow  them  to 
sit  or  lie  on  the  dtfmp  ground.  When  they  return,  if  the 
weather  be  cold,  they  should  be  encouraged  to  jump  and  run 
about ;  but  on  no  account  should  they  be  suffered  to  run  to  the 
fire,  a  practice  very  hurtful  in  many  respects. 

A  child's  maid,  or  a  nurse,  must  be  careful  never  to  bathe 
a  child  when  cold,  but  rather  when  it  feels  above  its  usual 
warmth.  Bathing  immediately  after  a  meal,  or  with  a  full 
stomach,  is  highly  improper  ;  nor  is  it  adviseable,  in  rough 
weather,  to  carry  a  child  into  the  open  air  too  soon  after 
bathing.  The  most  proper  time  for  using  the  bath  is  in  the 
evening,  when  the  child  can  be  removed  to  bed,  as  soon  as  he 
IS  completely  dried.  If  immersion  in  the  bath  be  quickly  fol- 
lowed by  a  glow  all  over  the  body,  and  a  perceptible  liveliness 
in  the  child,  we  may  be  sure  that  the  water  has  not  been  too 
cold  for  his  constitution,  and  that  we  have  proceeded  with  due 
care.  But  should  it  produce  chilliness,  evident  languor,  and 
depression,  we  must  make  the  water  a  little  wanner  next  time, 
and  not  venture  upon  the  cold  bath  till  we  are  encouraged  by 
more  favourable  appearances. 

All  the  benefit  of  the  bath  depends  upon  the  first  shock,  and 
the  reaction  of  the  system.  In  order  to  prevent  a  sudden  and 
strong  determination  of  the  blood  to  the  head,  it  is  always 
adviseable  to  dip  the  child  with  this  part  foremost,  and  to 
be  as  expeditious  as  possible  in  washing  away  all  impurities. 
The  whole  process  should  be  accompanied  with  lively  singing. 
It  is  of  fiir  greater  importance  than  most  people  may  be  aware 
of,  to  associate  in  early  life  the  idea  of  pleasure  and  cheerful- 
ness with  so  salutary  an  operation. 

During  the  use  of  the  lukewarm  bath,  the  whole  body  is  to 
be  immersed  in  it  every  night  as  well  as  morning.  But,  when 
recourse  is  had  to  cold  bathing,  it  must  be  used  in  the  manner 
above  prescribed  in  the  morning  only.    At  night,  it  will  be

Nurses  have  frequently  some  power  to  give  '  directions

seasoning,  all  sorts  of  pastry,  butter  in  every  form,  unripe  fruit, 
and  fermented  liquors  are  the  unfittest  and  heaviest  food  which 
can  be  given  to  children.  You  need  not  be  apprehensive  of 
giving  too  much  animal  food  to  the  children  as  they  grow  up, 
for  the  error  lies  gtfierally  the  contrary  way.  You  must  be 
careful  to  feed  your  charge  at  stated  and  regular  periods; 
.  perhaps  three  times  a  day  is  sufficient.  Feeding  children  at 
night  makes  them  fat  and  bloated.  Never  give  them  tta :  for 
this  shrub,  formed  in  an  abstract,  has  been  known  to  kill  in 
the  quantity  of  a  few  grains.  Dr.  Reece  relates,  that  tea 
brokers,  from  being  obliged  to  smell  the  boxes  containing  this 
herb,  generally  fall  victims  to  apoplexy ;  and  Dr.  Beddoes,  by 
a  variety  of  experiments,  found  its  efiects  equally  fatal  on  small 
animals.  How  powerfully  then  must  this  plant  afiect  the 
irritable  habit  of  children  ?  You  may,  however,  allow  them 
any  kind  of  mellow  fruit,  either  raw,  stewed,  or  baked ;  rool% 
of  all  sorts,  and  all  the  produce  of  the  kitchen  garden. .  *  Tht«e

a  pin*gtng,  when  it  might  fatigue  them  too  much  to  have  it

There  is  not  any  notion  which  is  more  difficult  to  root  out^ 
than  that  children  abound  with  ill  humours,  and  thai  these  can 
be  carried  off  only  by  purging  medietnes.  If  a  spot  appear  on 
the  skin,  the  child  must  have  his  guts  scoured  out,  to  mak« 
the  offensive  pimple  vanish.  They  little  know,  and  can  hardly 
be  made  to  conceive  that  all  purgatives^  however  mild  in  their 
operatim,  throw  the  stomach  into  immediate  disorder,  weaken 
its  digestive  powers,  vitiate  the  juices  designed  for  the  solution 
of  the  ik)od,  and  thus  prevent  the  due  preparation  of  the  chyle 
:whence  the  blood  is  formed.  This  is  the  sure  way  to  generate 
noxious  humours,  instead  of  expelling  them ;  and  to  taint  or 
impoverish  the  vital  stream,  instead  of  purifying  it.' 

A  medical  writer  <^  high  repute  says,  that  'a  timely  change 
in  the  diet  and  manner  of  life  may  prevent  many  dangerous 
dtsoirders.  The  first  tendency  to  disease  may  be  observed  in  a 
phikPs  breath.  It  is  not  enough  the  oreath  be  inoffensive :  it 
should  be  fresh  and  fragrant  like  a  nos^^y  of  fresh  flowers, 
or  a  pail  of  new  milk  from  a  young  cow  that  feeds  upon  the 
sweetest  grass  of  the  spring ;  and  this  as  well  at  first  waking 
in. the  morning,  as  all  day  long.  It  is  always  so  with  children 
that  are  in  perfect  delicate  health.  As  soon,  therefore,  as  a 
child's  breath  is  found  to  be  either  hot,  or  strong,  or  sour, 
we  may  be  assured  that  digestion  and  surfeit  have  fouled  and 
disturbed  the  blood,  and  now  is  the  time  to  apply  a  proper 


636  DOMESTIC  COOKERY. 

remedy^  and  prevent  a  traiti  of  impending  eviU.  Let  the  diild 
be  restrained  in  his  food ;  eat  less ;  live  upon  milk  or  thin  broth 
for  a  day  or  two ;  and  be  carried^  or  walk  if  he  is  able>  a  little 
more  than  usual  in  the  open  air. 

*  In  this  case  a  certain  fine  insipid  powder,  called  muignema 
alba,  is  recommended,  which,  at  the  same  time  itconectsand 
sweetens  all  sourness  rather  more  effectually  than  the  testa- 
ceous powders,  is  likewise  a  lenient  purgative,  and  keeps  tbe 
body  gently  open ;  not  that  physic  should  be  made  familiar ; 
but  one  dose  administered  now,  wOuld  prevent  the  necessitf  of 
a  great  many  that  might  afterwards  be  prescribed  with  much 
less  good  effect.  If  this  first  symptom  of  approadiing  illoen 
be  overlooked,  the  ehild,  who,  if  it  was  healthy,  would  lie  qaiet 
as  a  log  all  night,  will  have  disturbed  terrifying  dreams;  will 
be  talking,  starting,  kicking,  and  tumbling  about ;  or  smiliog 
and  laughing,  as  is  common  with  very  young  chfldren  when 
they  are  griped ;  mid  the  nurses  say  they  see  and  converse  with 
angels.  After  this  will  follow  loss  of  appetite  and  complexion, 
check  of  growth,  decay  of  strength,  cough,  consumption,  or 
else  colics,  gripes,  worms,  fits,  &c.  diseases  that  require  all  the 
skill  of  a  good  physician ;  and  happy  for  them,  if  the  utmost  be 
can  employ  will  restore  them. to  any  degree  of  lasting  health/ 

Children  are  often  lamed  and  maimed,  and  even  lose  their 
lives,  from  the  carelessness  or  ignorance  of  nurses.  When  a 
child's  clothes  catch  fire,  the  nurse  distracted  by  the  ftightfol 
scene,  and  the  cries  of  the  sufferer,  rushes  to  tear  them  off. 
But,  before  this^  can  be  effected,  the  mischief  is  done.  The 
attempt,  therefofg  should  never  be  made.  The  dothing, 
instead  of  being  torn  off,  ought  to  be  pressed  close  to  the  body, 
and  whatever  is  at  hand  wrapped  over  it,  so  as  to  exclude  the 
lur,  upon  which  the  blaze  will  go  out.  It  is  the  action  of  the 
air  that  keeps  it  alive,  and  increases  its  vehemence.  A  caipet> 
a  tablecloth,  a  blanket,  any  close  wrapper,  will  instantly  ex- 
tinguish it 

It  is  adviseable  to  have  children  taught  very  early  to  dread 
the  fire ;  and  the  best  way  of  impressing  their  minds  with  the 
danger  of  coming  too  near  it,  is  to  suffer  them  to  bom  their 
iingers  slightly,  yet  so  as  to  give  them  some  pain.  This  would 
have  more  effect  than  a  thousand  admonitions. 


ADVICB  TO  FBMALB  SERVANTS.  637 

Great  care  should  be  taken  that  nothing  that  is  hot  be  left 
within  a  child's  reach ;  otherwise  he  will  be  apt  to  pull  it  over 
iiim ;  in  which  case,  before  the  clothes  can  be  got  off,  he  may 
be  scalded  to  death.  Children  are  also  apt  to  carry  every  thing 
to  the  month ;  and  a  very  small  quantity  of  any  liquid  boil- 
ing hot,  will  occasion  death,  if  taken  into  the  stomach. 

It  would  be  tedious  to  enumerate  all  the  dangers  to  which 
cbildren  are  exposed,  but  a  good  nurse  will  foresee  and  provide 
against  the  most  common,  and  she  will  never  so  far  neglect  her 
doty,  as  to  permit  them  to  wander  alone  without  her  careful 
protection* 

A  nurse  should  alwajv  speak  truth  to  children,  always  per- 
form her  promise  to  them,  and  never  terrify  them  with  idle 
fears.  She  should  never  teach  them  to  resent  injuries,  or  to 
be  proud  of  their  clothes.  She  should  never  be  churlish,  nor 
too  fond,  but  always  treat  them  with  that  cheerfulness  and 
kindness,  which  they  will  soon  learn  to  imitate. 


DIRECTIONS  TO  DAIRY-MAIDS. 

• 

JL  HE  business  of  the  dairy-maid  is  of  the  most  beneficial 
nature,  as  by  her  knowledge  and  industry  we  ai^  furnished  with 
several  of  the  most  pleasant  and  essential  articles  of  food. 
We  shall  therefore  give  such  directions  as  may  enable,  the 
inexperienced  to  become  proper  proficients  in  so  valuable  an 
employment. 

ON  THE  MANAGEMENT  OF  THE  DAIRYi  COWS,  &C. 

The  greatest  possible  attention  must  be  paid  to  great  clean- 
liness in  a  dairy.  If  a  pump  can  be  fixed  in  the  place,  or  a 
stream  of  water  conveyed  through  it,  it  will  tend  to  preserve  a 
continued  freshness  and  purity  of  the  air.  The  floor  should  be 
neatly  paved  with  red  brick  or  smooth  stone,  and  laid  with  a 
proper  descent,  so  that  no  water  may  stagnate :  it  should  be 


flM  DOMESTIC  COOKBftY. 

yUrell  washed  eveiy  da\%  and  eold  water  diMira  over  ever]^|MiTt 
of  it  Tery  often,  and  all  the  atenaik,  sheUs,  and  dressers,  kqit 
vith  the  strictest  regard  to  cleanliness*  Neither  the  cheese 
and  rennetj  nor  the  cheese-press,  mdst'  be  suffered  io  ccMitract 
any  taint ;  nor  should  the  churns  be  scalded  in  the  dairy,  as 
the  steam  arising  from  hot  watei*  tends  greatly  to  injure  the 
milk.  There  should  be  shutters  to  keep  out  the  san  and  hot 
air.     Meat  hung  in  a  dairy  iivill  spoil  milk. 

The  utensils  of  the  dairy  shoilld  all  be  nsade  of  wood :  lead, 
topper,  and  brass  are  poisonous,  and  east  iron  gives  a  dis* 
agreeable  taste  to  the  productions  of  the  dairy.  MUk-hmiM  ia 
particular  should  be  utt^ly  abolished,  ahd  terthen  pans  well 
glased  used  in  tneir  stead.  Sour  milk  has  a  conpodiog  ten- 
deney ;  and  the  well  known  effects  of  the  poison  of  lead  are 
bodily  debility,  palsy,  and  death !  The  beat  of  all  aailk-vessds 
are  flat  wooden  trays,  about  three  inches  deep,  and  wideenougii 
to  contain  a  full  gallon  of  milk.  These  may  be  kept  pecftctly 
dean  with  good  care,  and  washing  and  scalding  them  well  with 
salt  and  water. 

When  the  milk  is  brought  into  the  dairy,  it  should  be 
strained  and  emptied  into '  clean  pans  immediately  in  winter, 
but  not  till  cool  in  summer.  White  ware  is  preferable,  as  the 
red  is  porous,  and  cannot  be  so  thoroughly  scalded. 

Cows  should  be  carefully  treated ;  if  their  teats  are  sore, 
they  should  be  soaked  in  warm  water  twice  a  day,  and  either 
be  dressed  with  soft  ointment,  or  done  with  spirits  and  water. 
If  the  former,  great  cleanliness  is  necessary.  The  milk,  at 
these  times,  should  be  given  to  the  pigs. 

The  eows  should  be  milked  at  a  regular  and  early  hour,  and 
the  udders  emptied,  or  the  quantity  wfll  decrease.  The  qoality 
of  milk  depends  on  many  causes ;  as  the  goodness,  breed,  and 
health  of  the  cow,  the  pasture,  the  length  of  time  from  <^vuig, 
the  having  plenty  of  clean  water  in  the  field  she  feeds  in,  &c. 
A  change  of  pastufe  will  tend  to  ii^crease  it.  People  who 
attend  properly  to  the  dairy  will  feed  the  cows  particularly 
well  two  or  three  weeks  before  they  calve,  which  makes  the 
milk  more  abundant  after.  In  gentlemen's  dairies  more  at- 
tention is  paid  to  the  size  and  beauty  of  the  cowa  than  to  their 
produce,  which  dairy-maids  look  most  to.

Foraoaking  diee§e  the  cows  should  calve  from  Lady^lay  to 
May*  that  thelarg«  quantity  of  milk  maj  come  into  use  about 
ttke  mane  time :  but  in  gentlemen's  families  one  or  two  should 
calve  in  August  or  September  for  a  supply  in  winter.  In  good 
paatiires,  jtfae  average  produce  of  a  dairy  is  about  three  gallons 
a  da|r  eadi  cow,  from  Lady^-day  to  Michaelmas^  and  from 
thence  to  Christmas  one  gallon  a  day.  Cows  will  be  profitable 
milkers  to  fourteen  or  fifteen  years  of  age^  if  a  proper  breed. 

When  a  calf  is  to  be  reared,  it  should  be  taken  from  the 
co'w  in  a  week  at  farthest^  or  it  will  cause  grieat  trouble  in

a  pan.  Take  it  from  the  cow  in  the  morning,  and  keep  iC 
without  food  till  Ae  next  morning ;  md  then,  being  hungry^ 
It  will  drink  without  difficulty.  Skimmed  milk  and  fresh 
Vrh^y,  just  as  warm  as  new  miUc,  should  be  given  twice  a  day 
in  such  quantity  as  is  required*  If  milk  runs  short,  smooth 
gruel  mixed  with  milk  will  do.  At  first  let  the  calf  be  out 
onlj  by  day,  and  feed  it  pight  and  morning. 

OBSERVATIONS  RESPECTING  CHEESE. 

This  well  known  article  differs  according  to  the  pasture  in 
which  the  cows  feed.  Various  modes  of  preparing  may  effect 
a  great  deal ;  and  it  will  be  bad  or  good  of  its  kind,  by  being 
in  unskilful  hands  or  the  contrary ;  but  much  will  still  depend 
on  the  former  circumstance.  The  same  land  rarely  makes 
very  fine  butter,  and  remarkably  fine  cheese;  yet  due  care 
may  give  one  pretty  good,  where  the  oth^  excels  in  quality. 
When  one  is  not  as  fine  as  the  other,  attention  and  change  of 
method  may  amend  the  inferior. 

Cheese  made  on  the  same  ground,  of  new,  skimmed,  or 
mixed  milk,  will  differ  greatly  not  in  richness  only,  but  also  in 
taste.  Those  who  direct  a  dairy  in  a  gentlmnan's  fiimily  should 
eonaider  in  what  way  it  can  be  mani^d  to  the  best  advantage. 
Even  with  lew  cows,  cheeses  of  value  xnsy  be  made  from  a 
tolerable  pasture,  by  taking  the  wh(^  of  two  meals  of  milk, 
and  proportioning  tb^  thickn^M  of  the  vaf  to  the  quantity, 
rather  than  having  a  wide  and  flat  one,  as  the  former  will  be 
morp  mellow*  Xhe  fiddition  of  a  pound  of  fresh  made  butter, 
of  a  good  quality,  will  cause  the  clieese  iQade  on  poor  land  to 
be  of  a  very  different  quality  from  that  usually  produced  by  it. 


040  DOMK8TIC  COOKERY. 

A  few  cheeses  thus  made,  when  the  weather  b  not  extrane- 
ly  hotj  and  when  the  cows  are  in  full  feed,  will  be  very  advan- 
tageous  for  the  use  of  .the  parlour.  Cheese  for  common  fimiiij 
use  will  be  very  well  produced  by  two  meals  of  skim,  and  one 
of  new  milk ;  or  in  good  land,  by^  skim  milk  only.  Butter 
likewiae  should  be  made,  and  potted  down  for  winter  use,  bat 
not  to  interfere  with  the  cheese  as  above,  which  will  not  take 
much  time. 

To  prepare  Rennet  to  turn  the  Milk. 

Take  out  the  stomach  of  a  calf  as  soon  as  killed,  and  scour 
it  inside  and  out  with  salt,  after  it  is  cleaned  of  the  curd  always 
found  in  it.  Let  it  drain  a  few  hours;  then  sew  it  up  with 
two  good  handfuls  of  salt  in  it,  or  stretch  it  on  a  stick  wdl 
salted ;  or  keep  it  in  the  salt  wet,  and  soak  a  bit,  which  wiH 
do  over  and  over  by  fresh  water. 

To  make  Cheese. 

Put  the  milk  into  a  large  tub,  warming  a  part  till  it  is  of  a 
degree  of  heat  quite  equal  to  new ;  if  too  hot  the  cheese  will 
be  tough.  Put  as  much  rennet  as  will  turn  it,  and  cover  it 
over.  .  Let  it  stand  till  completely  turned ;  then  strike  the 
curd  down  several  times  with  the  skimming  dish,  and  let  it 
separate,  still  covering  it.  There  are  two  modes  of  breaking 
die  curd ;  and  there  will  be  a  difierence  in  the  taste  of  the 
cheese,  according  as  either  is  observed ;  one  is  to  gather  It  with 
the  hands  very  gently  towards  the  side  of  the  tub,  letting  the 
whey  pass  through  the  fingers  till  it  Is  cleared,  and  lading  It  off 
as  it  collects.  The  other  is,  to  get  the  whey  from  it  by  eariy 
breaking  the  curd ;  the  last  method  deprives  it  of  many  of  its 
oily  particles,  and  is  therefore  less  proper. 

'  Put  the  vat  or  ladder  over  the  tub,  and  fiU  it  with  curd  by 
the  skimmer :  press  the  curd  close  with  your  hand,  and  add 
more  as  it  sinks:  and  it  must  be  finally  left  two  inches  above 
the  edge.  Before  the  vat  is  filled,  the  cheese-cloth  must  be 
laid  at  the  bottom ;  and  when  full,  drawn  smooth  over  on  all 
sides. 

There  are  two  modes  of  saltuig  cheese :  one  by  mixing  it  m 
the  curd  while  in  the  tub  after  the  whey  Is  out ;  and  the  other

DeHcate  Cream  Ckeese. 

Take  to  every  quart  of  new  milk  a  gill  of  cream,  make  tbe 
mixture  slightly  warm,  and  put  into  it  as  little  rennet  as  miy 
be  necessary  just  to  turn  it.  The  curd  being  come,  to  use  the 
language  of  the  dairy,  lay  a  cloth  on  the  vat  or  .«ieadd,  fdiidi 
may  be  the  bottom  of  a  sieve,  but  should  be  'the  exact  aiae  of 
the  intended  cheese ;  then,  cutting  out  the  curd  with  a  akkn- 
ming  dish,  fill  up*  the  mould,  turn  the  doth  over  it,  and  leave 
it  to  drain.  As  the  curd  drains  and  settles,  keep  filling  in  moR 
with  a  gentle  pressure,  till  all  the  whey  is  out,  and  there  it 
sufficient  substance  for  the  cheese.  It  must  be  then  tomed  isto 
a  dry  cloth,  and  pressed  witii  a  moderate  weight,  not  exceed- 
ing two  pounds.  At  night  it  is  to  be  turned  into  a  dean  doth; 
and,  tbe  next  morning,  very  slightly  qpxinkled  with  fine  tth: 
after  which,  if  suffidentiy  dry,  it  may  be  laid  on  a  bedof  feh 
nettie,  strawberry  or  ash  leaves ;  oovored  over  with  more ;  and, 
being  shifted  and  turned  twice  a  day,  having  the  leaves  ooo- 
sionally  renewed,  will  in  less  than  a  fortnight  be  suffidentiy 
ripened  for  eating.  If  expedition  be  desirable,  the  maturity 
of  the  chieese  may  be  considei^ably  hastened  by  keeping  it  in  a 
warm  place,  between  two  pewter  dishes,  and  giving  it  a  fresh 
bed  and  covering  of  leaves  every  day. 

Net  Ckteu. 

There  are  considerable  quantities  of  net  cheeses  made  in 
North  Wiltshire;  which  are  prepared^  in  all  respects,  eicq^t 
pressing,  like  the  other  best  cheeses.  It  is  a  remarkable  dr- 
cumstanoe,  and  highly  deserving  of  attention,  that  tbeae  net 
cheeses  are  never  dther  hoven  or  eyed,  defects  fiequently  itivxA 


ADVIOX  TO  FEMALE  SERVANTS. 

in  all  die  oAmt  sorts.    It  is,  in  &ct,  no  uncommon  circum* 
•tanoey  for  the  same  maker  to  form,  out  of  one  oowl  of  curd, 
pressed  cheeses  which  heave,  and  net  cheeses  which  are  per- 
fectly «lose.    There  seems,  therefore,  but  little  occasion  to  look 
farther  for  a  cause.     In  making  net  cheeses,    the  curd   is 
aqneesed  as  closely  and  tightly  as  possible  into  the  nets  by  hand, 
l>ut  receives  no  other  compression;  in  other  cheeses,  the  curd 
is  subjected  to  the  powers  of  a  press :  and  it  has  not  failed  to 
be  noticed  that,  the  heavier  the  press,  the  greater  is  the  pro- 
pensity of  the  cheeses  to  heaving,  as  well  as  that  a  similar 
effect  is  produced  by  overfilling  the  vats.     The  natural  infer- 
ence is,  that  an  instrument  of  pressure  as  nearly  as  possible  on 
the  principle  of  that  of  the  hand,  with  powers  suited  to  the 
size  of  the  largest  cheese,  would  in  like  manner  render  cheeses 
of,  any  form  or  magnitude  invariably  close ;  if,  indeed,  there 
should  not  also  be  a  necessity  of  having  the  mould  or  vat  so 
ooostructed  as  to  leave  the  sides  of  the  cheese  seme  degree  of 
freedom  like  the  meshes  of  a  net. 

To  make  Cheeu  taund. 

Wash  in  warm  whey,  when  you  have  any,  and  wipe  it  once 
a  month,  and  keep  it  on  a  rack.  If  you  want  to  ripen  it,  a 
damp  cellar  will  bring  it  forward.  When  a  whole  cheese  is 
cut,  the  larger  quantity  should  bespread  with  butter  inside, 
and  the  outside  wiped,  to  preserve  it.  To  keep  those  in  daily 
use  moist,  let  a  dean  cloth  be  rung  out  from  cold  water,  and 
wrapped  round  them  when  carried  from  table.  Dry  cheese 
may  be  used  to  advantage  to  grate  for  serving  with  macaroni, 
creating  without.  These  observations  are  made  with  a  view 
to  make  the  above  articles  less  expensive,  as  in  most  families 
where  much  is  used  there  is  waste.

To  preserve  Butter  far  IVinter,  the  best  Way. 
When  the  butter  has  been  prepared  as  above  directed,  take 
two  parts  of  the  best  common  salt,  one  part  of  good  loaf-sugar, 
and  one  part  of  saltpetre,  beaten  and  blended  well  together. 
Of  this  composition  put  one  ounce  to  sixteen  ounces  of  butter, 
and  work  it  well  together  in  a  mass.  Press  it  into  the  pans 
after  the  butter  is  become  cool ;  for  friction,  though  it  be  not

touched  by  die  hands/  will  aoftai  it.  The  pms  should  hold 
•ten  or  twelve  pounds  each.  On  the  top  put  sooie  salt ;  and

Buttermilk. 

Bttttermilky  if  made  of  sweet  cream,  is  a  delicii>iu  and  BMnt 
wholesome  food.  Those  who  can  relish  soor  buttermilk  find  it 
still  more  light;  and  it  is  reckoned  more  beneficial  in  con- 
sumptive cases. 

Buttermilk^  if  not  very  sour,  is  also  as  good  as  cream  to 
eat  with  fruit,  if  sweetened  with  white  sugar,  and  mixed  widi 
a  very  little  milk.  It  likewise  does  equally  for  cakes  and  noe- 
puddings,  and  of  course  it  is  economical  to  chum  before  the 
cream  is  too  stale  for  any  thing  but  to  feed  pigs. 

To  keep  Milk  and  Cream* 

In  hot  weather,  when  it  is  difficult  to  preserve  milk  fifom 
becoming  sour,  and  spoiling  the  cream,  it  may  be  kept  perfectly 
sweet  by  scalding  the  new  milk  very  gently  without  boilings 
and  setting  it  by  in  the  earthen  dish  or  pan  that  it  is  done  in. 
This  method  is  pursued  in  Devonshire ;  and  for  batter,  and 
eatbg,  would  equally  anawer  in  small  quantities  for  cofie,  tea, 
.&c.  Cream  ^eady  skimmed  may  be  kept  twenty-fimr  honrs 
if  scalded  without  sugar ;  and  by  adding  to  it  as  much  pow- 
dered lump^ugar  as  shall  make  it  pretty  sweety  will  be  good 
two  days,  keeping  it  in  a  tool  plaoe.

MANAOSMBNT  OF  FOWLS. 

J[N  order  to  have  fine  fowls,  it  is  necessary  to  choose  a  good 
breed,  and  have  proper  care  taken  of  them.  People  differ  in 
their  opinions  of  which  is  best.  The  black  are  very  juicy ;  but 
do  not  answer  so  well  for  boiling,  as  their  legs  partake  of  their 
colour.  They  should  be  fed  as  nearly  as  possible  It  the  same 
hour  and  place.  Potatoes  boiled,  unskinned,  in  a  little  water, 
and  then  cut,  and  either  wet  with  skimmed  ipilk  or  not,  form 
one  of  the  best  foods.  Turkeys  and  fowls  thrive  amazingly  on 
them.     The  milk  must  not  be  sour. 

The  best  age  for  setting  a  hen  is  from  two  to  five  years  ; 
and  you  should  remark  which  hens  make  the  best  brooders, 
and  keep  those  to  laying  which  are  giddy  and  careless  of  their 
young.  In  justice  to  the  animal  creation,  however,  it  must  be 
observed,  there  are  but  few  instances  of  bad  parents  for  the 
time  their  nursing  is  necessary. 

Hens  sit  twenty  days.  Convenient  places  should  be  pro* 
vided  for  their  laying,  as  those  will  be  proper  ibr  sitting 
likewise.  If  the  hen-house  is  not  secured  from  vermin,  the 
eggs  wfll  be  sucked. 

Those  hens  are  usually  preferred  which  have  tufts  of  feathers 
on  their  heads  ^  those  that  crow  are  not  looked  upon  as  profit- 
able. Some  fine  young  fowls  should  be  reared  every  year,  to 
keep  up  a  stock  of  good  breeders;  and  by  this  attention,  and 
removing  bad  layers  and  careless  nurses,  you  will  have  a 
chance  of  a  good  stock. 

Let  the  hens  lay  some  time  before  you  set  them,  which 
should  be  done  firom  the  end  of  February  to  the  beginning  of 
May.  Wiiile  hens  are  laying/  feed  them  well,  and  sometimes 
with  oats. 

Broods  of  chickens  are  hatched  all  through  the  summer,  but 
those  that  come  out  very  late,  require  much  time  till  they  have 
gained  some  strength.

Ducks* 

Dudes  generally  begin  to  lay  in  the  month  of  February. 
'Their  eggs  should  be  daily  taken  away  from  them,  till  they 
•eem  inclined  to  sit;  then  leave  them  and  see  that  there  are

enough.  They  reqone  no  attention  while  ritthig»  except  to 
give  them  food  «t  the  time  they  come  out  to  seek  it ;  end  there 
should  be  water  placed  at  a  moderate  diatanoe  from  them,  tfiat 
their  eggs  may  not  foe  spoiled  by  their  long  absence  in  seeking 
it.  Twelve  or  thirteen  eggs  are  enough ;  in  an  early  season  it 
is  best  to  set  tbem  undfer  a  hen ;  and  then  they  can  be  kept 
from  water  till  they  have  a  little  istrength  td  bear  it,  whi(&  in  very 
cold  weather  they  cannot  do  so  well*  They  should  be  put 
under  cover,  especially  in  a  wet  season ;  for  though  water  is 
the  natural  element  of  ducks,  yet  they  are  apt  to  be  killed  by 
ihe  cramp  before  ihey  are  coVered  witb  feathers  to  de&nd 
them. 

Ducks  should  be  accustomed  to  feed  and  rest  at  one  place. 
which  would  prevent  their  straggling  too  fer  to  lay.  Places 
near  the  water  to  lay  in  ar^  advantageous ;  and  those  might  be 
small  wooden  houscfs,  with  a  partition  in  the  middle^  and  a 
door  at  each  end.  They  eat  any  thing;  and  when  to  be 
fattened,  must  have  plenty,  however  coarse^  and  in  tfiree 
weeks  they  will  be  fiit. 

Turkeys. 

Turkeys  are  Very  tender  when  young.  As  so6n  as  hatched 
put  three  peppercorns  down  their  throat  Great  care  is  neces- 
sary to  their  wdl-foeing,  because  the  hen  is  so  careless  that  she 
will  walk  about  with  one  chick,  and  leave  the  remainder,  or 
even  tread  upon  and  kill  them.  Turkeys  are  violent  esters,  aud 
jaittst  therefore  be  left  to  take  charge  of  themselves  in  general, 
except  one  good  feed  a  day.  The  hen  sits  twen^-five  or 
thirty  days ;  and  the  young  opes  must  be  kept  warm,  as  the 
least  cold  or  damp  kills  them.  They  must  be  fed  often;  and 
at  a  distance  from  the  hen,  which  will  eat  every  thing  from 
them.  Tbey  should  have  curds,  green  cheese  parmga  cut 
emiill,  and  bread  and  milk  with  chopped  wormwood  in  it ;  and 
.their  drink  milk  and  waur,  but  not  left  ta  be  sour.  All  young 
fowls  are  a  prey  for  vermin,  therefore  they  should  be  kept  in  a 
safe  place  where  none  can  come ;  weasels,  stoats,  ferrets,  &c 
creep  in  at  very  small  crevices. 

Let  the  hen  be  under  a  coop,  in  a  warm  place  exposed  to 
the  sun,  for  the  first  three  or  four  weeks ;  and  the  young  shodd 
not  be  sufifered  to  go  out  in  the  dew  at  morning  or  evening.

Twehre  eggs  «re  tnough  to  put  under  a  turkey ;  and.  when 
she  is  about  to  1ay»  lock  her  up  till  she  has  laid  every  morning. 
They  usually  begin  to  lay  in  March,  uid  sit  in  April.  Feed 
them  near  the  hen-house ;  and  giye  them  a  little  meat  in  the 
evening,  to  accustom  them  to  roosting  there.  Fatten  them 
with  sodden  oats  or  barley  for  the  first  fortnight,  and  the  last 
fortnight  give  them  as  above,  and  rice  svrelled  with  warm  milk 
over  the  fire  twice  a  day.  The  flesh  will  be  beautifully  whitis 
and  fine  flavoured.  The  common  way  is  to  cram  them,  but 
th^  are  so  ravenous  that  it  seems  unnecessary,  if  they  are  not 
suffered  to  go  fiur  from  home,  which  makes  them  poor. 

Pea  Fowl. 

Feed  them  as  you  do  turkeys.  They  are  so  shy  that  they 
are  seldom  found  for  some  days  after  hatching :  and  it  is  very 
wrong  to  pursue  them,  as  many  ignorant  people  do,  in  the  idea 
of  bringing  them  home ;  for  it  only  causes  the  hen  to  carry  the 
young  ones  through  dangerous  places,  and,  by  hurrying,  she 
treads  upon  them.  The  cock  kills  all  the  young  chickens  he 
can  get  at,  by  one  blow  bn  the  centre  of  the  head  with  his  bill; 
and  he  does  the  saiiie  by  his  own  brood  before  the  feathers  of 
the  crown  come  out.  Nature  therefore  impels  the  hen  to  keep 
them  out  of  his  way  till  the  feathers  vise. 

Guinea  Hens. 

Chiinea  hens  lay  a  great  number  of  eggs ;  and  if  you  can 
discover  the  nest  it  is  best'  to  put  them  under  common  hens, 
which  are  better  nurses.  They  require  great  warmth,  quiet, 
and  careful  feeding,  with  rice  swelled  with  milk,  or  bread 
aoidced  in  it.  Put  two  peppercorns  down  their  throat  when 
first  hatched. 

Pigeons* 

They  bring  two  young  ones  at  a  time ;  and  breed  every 
month,  if  well  looked  after,  and  plentifully  fed.  They  should 
be  kept  very  clean,  and  the  bottom  of  the  dove>cot  be  strewed 
with  sand .  once  a  month  at  least.  Tares  and  white  peas  are 
their  proper  food.  They  should  have  plenty  of  fresh  water 
in  their  house.  Starlingf  an^^  other  hints  are  apt  to  come 
among  them,  and  suck  the  eggs.    Vermin  likewise  are  their.

MEANS  OF  DE8TR0TI90  NO»OV8  INSECTS,  &C.     fU 


MEANS  OF  DEgTROYINO  NOXIOUS  INSECTS,  Sto. 

'IJIVILIZATION  and  the  arts  having  made  the  desert  ta 
blossom  as  the  rose^  Have  alse  delivered  u%  from  the  power 
of  ravenous  beasts;  but  we  are  etill  liable  to  be  attacked  by  a 
more  numerooa  though  less  powerful  host  of  enemTes,  who 
commit  their  depredations  on  the  animal  and  vegetable  kingw 
doms,  and  thereby  destroy  many  of  the  comforts  of  human  life. 
Dwelling  houses  are  continaally  exposed  to  the  invasion  of 
insects^  reptiles,  and  other  kinds  of  vermin,  whose  uses  la 
creation  we  have  either  not  discovered,  or  whose  exterminatioi^ 
is  left  to  the  power  and  wisdom  of  man  to  accomplish.  Jt 
doeMl  not  become  us  to  be  prodigal  of  life  in  any  form,  nor 
wantonly  to  seek  its  destruction;  but  when  any  species  of 
l^nimftlg  become  reidly  noxious,  the  good  of  creation  requirea 
that  they  should  be  destroyed. 

BeetkM. 

To  destroy  beetles  that  are  troublesome  in  the  house,  pttt 
some  small  lumps  of  quick  lime  info  the  chinks  or  holes  of  the 
vrsil  frots  whence  tfacy  issue,  <Mr  seattet  it  on  the  ground*  Or 
ttt  kught,  hiy  a  little  treade  on  a  piece  of  wood,  and  float  it  in 
B  psn  ef  water :  beel^a  are  so  fond  of  treads  that  they  w^  be 
drowned  in  attempting  to  get  at  it.  The  common  black  beette 
may  be  also  extirpated  by  placing  a  hedge-hog  in  the  room 
during  the  summer  nights ;  or  by  placing  a  bundle  of  pea  straw 
aear  Uieir. holes,  imd  afterwards  burning  it  when  the  beetles 
-faa?e  crept  into  it*

Bugs*

ef  Termin.  Dip  a  sponf^  or  brush  into  the  sdstion,  and  rub
